entries.title: Cubs’ offense backs Jameson Taillon's solid start
entries.summary: <p>SAN DIEGO — Jameson Taillon spent part of Wednesday morning giving an impromptu concert with guitars stashed in the visitors’ clubhouse at Petco Park.</p><p>Given how well he pitched, setting down the first 14 batters in order, surviving two solo homers, and then allowing just one more hit in seven innings of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a>’ 5-4 victory over the Padres, should we expect an encore from the music man?</p><p>“No, there’s no guitars anywhere else other than here,’’ Taillon said. “So we’re safe.</p><p>“They’re Taylor guitars, really, really nice, actually. I mean, I haven’t played in like 10 years, but I used to play a bunch, and I know about 4½ songs.’’</p><p>His signature tune?</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatRight><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/C-Naa1HXeDQ?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Matchbox Twenty - 3AM (Official Video) [HD Remaster]"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>“3AM by Matchbox Twenty,’’ he said.</p><p>Fourteen up, fourteen down is generally too early to be thinking about 27 up, 27 down. Taillon was aware that no one had reached base. “More than anything, I was aware I had good stuff today and good command, good pitch mix,’’ he said.</p><p>The Cubs and Padres played each other in a NL wild-card series last October, the Cubs taking two out of three, with all three games played in Wrigley because the Cubs finished with a better regular season record. Taking two out of three in Petco, and finishing 3-3 in this six-game trip against the best in the West, the Dodgers and Padres, even if it’s April, made for a satisfying flight home.</p><p>Ben Brown assured the Cubs of a happy landing by inheriting a bases-loaded, no-out situation in the eighth and allowing just one run, on a sacrifice fly by Fernando Tatis Jr. Brown subsequently induced a double-play ground ball from Manny Machado, stand-in second baseman Matt Shaw turning two to end the inning.</p><p>Home runs by Shaw, who fell a triple short of the cycle (bunt single in the fourth, double in the sixth), and Pete Crow-Armstrong, his second in two games, proved decisive.</p><p>“I mean, he impacts the game no matter what,’’ Taillon said. “Even if the bat’s not there, he’s making a difference running the bases and playing defense with the energy he brings, but when the bat’s going, it’s a game changer. He can electrify an offense.’’</p><p>Former Cubs pitcher and broadcaster Rick Sutcliffe said he spoke with injured pitcher Justin Steele [strained flexor] by phone Wednesday. “We talked a little, cried a little,’’ Sutcliffe said. “He had an injection. They’ll have to wait three or four weeks for the inflammation to go down. Things were going so well. I just told him this team is built for October. Get back here by September 1 and get after it.’’</p><p>Padres pitchers threw at least five pitches that were dangerously wild the last two games. Shaw was knocked down Tuesday and had a pitch go behind him Wednesday, Seiya Suzuki was knocked down Tuesday, and Nico Hoerner was hit by a pitch in the brim of his helmet. “I’ve never seen so many balls high and tight in a series, right?” Shaw said. “I mean, there’s certain times maybe guys are doing it on purpose, but it was never like that here today. You just got to get back up and play the game.’’</p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-hide-descriptions="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-c90005" name="list-c90005"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, count=13868, titleUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs, inverseColors=false, footerBgColor=#000000, title=Latest on the Cubs, bodyFont=font-3, showListNumbers=false, imagePositionHorizontal=null, rteBodyFont=font-3, headerBottomBorderColor=#111010, subTitle=null, stickyHeader=true, listTitleColor=#000000, tableCellBorderColor=#ccd1d5, relatedListFont=font-1, hideAuthors=true, tertiaryTextColor=#6c6c6c, buttonHoverTextColor=#ffffff, tableFont=font-1, carouselAutoPlay=false, promoTitleFont=font-2, ctaButtonText=[], primaryTextColor=#2a2a2a, buttonTextColor=#ffffff, linkTextColor=#1a1a1a, hideTitles=false, footerLinkColor=#ffffff, items=[{type=Image, categoryUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs, media=[{image={src=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/10/9b/621aff344eacaf6e2bdca2a2695f/phillies-cubs-baseball.jpg, width=5277, height=3518}, ampLink=null, _styledTemplate=null, _template=/image/Image.hbs, narrowImage={src=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/10/9b/621aff344eacaf6e2bdca2a2695f/phillies-cubs-baseball.jpg, width=5277, height=3518}, alt=Phillies Cubs Baseball}], _styledTemplate=null, imageAlignment=null, isFeatured=false, title=Cubs manager Craig Counsell celebrates 900th MLB victory, publishDate=April 29, 2026 10:28 PM, timestamp=1777519712000, description=[{items=[Counsell credits former players and coaches on his career mark. entries.title: Cubs manager Craig Counsell celebrates 900th MLB victory
entries.summary: <p>SAN DIEGO — The bitter taste of victory?</p><p>How else to describe the shot of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/7/8/23787109/malort-jeppsons-unlikely-rise-liquor-alcohol-wormwood" >Jeppson’s Malört </a>that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> players and staff insisted Craig Counsell share with them on the occasion of his 900th win as a major-league manager Tuesday night.</p><p>A helpful member of the Cubs’ traveling party made sure to have the libation on hand, which has become<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/entertainment-and-culture/2021/8/25/22641237/what-does-malort-taste-like-chicago-liquor" > a cult favorite</a> in Chicago establishments that serve adult beverages, even though it has been described as a “famously disgusting liqueur from Chicago.”</p><p>Counsell, a Wisconsinite savvy enough to avoid offending the local citizenry, did not share his opinion. Instead, he offered a couple of thoughts on what it meant to reach the round number that leaves him No. 78 on the all-time managers list — nine wins behind Jimy Williams after win No. 901 on Wednesday — and fifth on the list of active managers (The Reds’ Terry Francona ranks No. 1 with 2,051 entering Wednesday).</p><p>“Mostly, I think you think about players No. 1, and then probably that you’ve just been in the game for a long time,’’ he told the reporters gathered in his office. “You’ve been doing it for a while, and fortunate to be doing it for a long time.</p><p>“. . . You think of players going through this journey of their career, like [reliever] Ryan Rolison getting their shot in the last couple of days and doing a heck of a job. Those are the rewarding moments. . . . Those are the things that you’re happy about and proud of as you go through this.’’</p><p>Earlier Tuesday, Counsell sat on a dugout bench and talked about those who helped him along the way — Pat Murphy, his coach at Notre Dame and then his bench coach in Milwaukee; Jim Leyland, his first manager in the big leagues; and Bob Melvin, who managed him in Arizona.</p><p>“[Melvin] let me, you know, behind the curtain,’’ Counsell told the Sun-Times, “a little bit into what his job was like. He gave me a lot of freedom as a player. And he always talked<br>to me about why you did it and why you don’t, what he was thinking. He definitely exposed me to a little more.’’</p><p>Counsell is 55 now, an age where you are more likely to be mentor than mentee.</p><p>“I think when you get older, you look forward to sharing more, but you’re still learning,’’ said Counsell, who received congratulations from several members of the managing fraternity, including Alex Cora.</p><p>“So you’re still learning a lot, so I don’t think that changes. There’s still things you can learn and be better at. That never stops.’’</p><p>There are no seminars or weekend retreats for big-league managers. Generally, the only time they’re all together in one place is during the winter meetings, but much of that time is spent plotting trades and free-agent signings.</p><p>Counsell said he talks fairly regularly with other managers by phone.</p><p>“You’re definitely in relationships with some of these guys,’’ he said. “. . . If you’ve this done for a while, maybe you’re not close friends with some of these guys, but you’re checking on them, they’re checking on you, and you run questions by them.’’</p><p>Then there are interactions with coaches from other sports. Counsell tends to favor basketball coaches. While in San Diego, he visited with the basketball coaches at the University of San Diego.</p><p>“I think the coaches from other sports, they share a lot of the same concepts,’’ he said. “You’re managing people, you’re managing up [to management] and down [to players], so to speak. That’s probably the biggest commonality.’’</p><p>He finds material on social media posted by coaches useful. “Like Kara Lawson, the women’s basketball coach at Duke,’’ he said. “She’s awesome with stuff.’’</p><p>Counsell is not into reading leadership or self-help books. “When I read,’’ he said, “it’s usually fiction. I do it more to get away from the game.’’</p><p>As for the current emphasis on analytics? “I don’t like that word,’’ he said. “I prefer calling it information. How are people using information.’’</p><p>Absorbing the information, then conveying it. He finds that the degree in accounting he earned at Notre Dame has proven useful. How good an accountant would he have been?</p><p>“It wasn’t a life goal to be an accountant,’’ he said.</p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-hide-descriptions="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-c90005" name="list-c90005"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, count=13868, titleUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs, inverseColors=false, pagination=[{body=[Next Page], href=?p=2, _template=/link/Link.hbs, contentId=null, pianoSectionSlug=null, target=null}], pullQuoteIcon=[{image=null, narrowImage=null, _template=/image/Image.hbs, alt=null}], footerBgColor=#000000, title=Latest on the Cubs, bodyFont=font-3, showListNumbers=false, imagePositionHorizontal=null, rteBodyFont=font-3, headerBottomBorderColor=#111010, subTitle=null, stickyHeader=true, listTitleColor=#000000, tableCellBorderColor=#ccd1d5, relatedListFont=font-1, hideAuthors=true, tertiaryTextColor=#6c6c6c, buttonHoverTextColor=#ffffff, tableFont=font-1, carouselAutoPlay=false, promoTitleFont=font-2, ctaButtonText=[], primaryTextColor=#2a2a2a, buttonTextColor=#ffffff, linkTextColor=#1a1a1a, hideTitles=false, footerLinkColor=#ffffff, items=[{type=Image, description=[{items=[Counsell credits former players and coaches on his career mark. entries.author: Gordon Edes

entries.title: After two years in Chicago, the Angel Reese era now feels like a dream
entries.summary: <p>Was it all a dream?</p><p>When Angel Reese was drafted No. 7 in 2024, it felt like a new day in Chicago. The world's attention was turning toward women's basketball, and now, blessed with one of the sport's biggest names, Chicago would be at the epicenter.</p><p>Reese had her naysayers coming out of LSU, but she proved many of them wrong.</p><p>Her game, it turned out, translated to the pros. In her rookie season, she surpassed Sylvia Fowles to claim the single-season rebounding record and set a WNBA mark for the most consecutive double-doubles with 15.</p><p>There was still a lot of room for growth, sure, but it looked like she had the potential to be the face of the franchise. </p><p>It wasn't just that she was a generational rebounder with All-Defense potential and MVP aspirations.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/29/angel-reese-is-living-the-dream-in-atlanta-and-this-match-might-be-built-to-last" >Greenberg: Angel Reese is living the dream in Atlanta — and this match might be built to last</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>It was also that she was the kind of larger-than-life figure the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> had not had since Candace Parker. She was the kind of star who ensured celebrities sat courtside at Wintrust — the kind who could hit the Met Gala on a Monday and help down the Liberty on a Tuesday.</p><p>That meant something. Players who change franchises don't just produce; they project their personality. Reese had the necessary flair: she was unapologetic, multi-dimensional, a fierce competitor.</p><p>There was a certain grace in the fact that she wanted to be in Chicago. The Sky had struggled since the 2021 championship core split and head coach James Wade departed abruptly during the 2023 season. But Reese embraced the opportunity. She wanted to play for Teresa Weatherspoon, a WNBA legend and first-time head coach, and the city rallied around them in 2024.</p><p>But the fame and the spotlight Reese carries also embedded a tension into the partnership with the Sky. While Reese pulled Chicago into the WNBA's modern era, the Sky remained relative stragglers on the organizational side.</p><p>As franchises in New York, Phoenix, Minnesota and Las Vegas began operating more like NBA teams, the Sky still lacked a dedicated practice facility, a robust performance staff, and an in-house analytics team. </p><p>Still, Reese defended them. She told reporters at media day in 2025 that while people want to start with the "glitz and the glamour," you sometimes have to "start in the gutter."</p><p>Maybe Reese and the Sky could evolve together. Growing pains and all.</p><p>Reese's second season had plenty of them. She endured an epic slump to start the year, struggling to finish around the rim and looking hesitant with her outside shot. But she played herself out of it, adapting to coach Tyler Marsh's system and showing she could be a playmaker and a ballhandler, too.</p><p>Meanwhile, the Sky were headed toward one of their worst seasons in franchise history, weighed down by injuries and a talent deficit. Organizationally, they were trying to elevate the player experience, but the reality of practicing in a public recreation center overshadowed other changes.</p><p>By the end of the season, Reese's public comments suggested she was far less satisfied with the view from the gutter.</p><p>On the Sky's facilities: "Y'all saw the rec where we practice at … I don't think anyone wants to practice there."</p><p>On their roster: "I'm not settling for the same s— we did this year," Reese told the Tribune. "We have to get good players. We have to get great players. That's a non-negotiable for me."</p><p>The Sky ultimately suspended Reese for a half-game for comments she made in the Tribune interview, and she sat out the remainder of the season with a back injury.</p><p>The relationship hit a crossroads. Could Reese still be a foundational piece? A pillar? Somewhere in between? Then, poof, she was gone.</p><p>To hear both sides tell it now, it is almost as if the era never happened. Both insist it was a mutual parting: no trade request, no love lost. Just a general manager <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/22/will-this-time-be-different-sky-push-for-a-fresh-start" >making an opportunistic move</a>, just a player doing what was best for her career.</p><p>"There's a lot of gray area in basketball and trading and there's no black and white answer of this is right and this is wrong," Sky guard Hailey Van Lith said before Wednesday's preseason game against the Dream. "People just go with their gut and I think that's kind of what played out. It's a gray area, and you have respect for everyone involved and you keep pushing."</p><p>The Dream won the gray area Wednesday night, 87-78, with Reese looking like what her new teammates have been calling "the missing piece." </p><p>What's possible for this Dream team in the regular season?</p><p>"I think what's possible is ultimately win a championship," coach Karl Smesko said before the game.</p><p>What's possible for the Sky?</p><p>It's still tough to get a good read. On paper they look like a playoff-caliber team, but three of their major offseason signings—Azurá Stevens, Courtney Vandersloot and DiJonai Carrington—are all out with injuries and lack specific return timelines.</p><p>The Sky do have candidates for the face-of-the-franchise question in Rickea Jackson and Kamilla Cardoso. They’ve also taken steps to professionalize, now practicing at UIC’s Flames Athletic Center and adding a performance staff.</p><p>But there's plenty of gray area left to work through.</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" entries.title: Becoming Bovino
entries.author: WBEZ

entries.title: Angel Reese is living the dream in Atlanta — and this match might be built to last
entries.summary: <p>No defensiveness. No dismissiveness. No unpleasantness.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/28/sky-angel-reese-strike-friendly-tone-ahead-of-preseason-matchup" target="_blank" >Angel Reese</a> answered the questions that had to be asked Wednesday, and the new face of the Atlanta Dream handled them like a pro’s pro.</p><p>She can’t be thrilled with how her two years with the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> ended — after all that losing and with <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2025/09/05/sky-suspend-angel-reese-for-first-half-of-aces-game" target="_blank" >acrimony between superstar and team</a> — can she?</p><p>“I’m still happy,” she said. “I’m grateful for my time here in Chicago — I experienced a lot of great things — but I’m just happy to start this new journey.”</p><p>Why didn’t things work out better between the “Bayou Barbie” and the team that drafted her in 2024, which she publicly took to task for some of its glaring weaknesses late last season?</p><p>“I think it was just best for me to move on, and I think it was helpful for both of us,” she said. “I’m grateful for Chicago for putting me in a [trade with Atlanta] where I can come somewhere that I really wanted and people really wanted me. I’m just grateful to turn the page. No love lost.”</p><p>Did returning to Wintrust Arena for an exhibition game in her old backyard remind her just how permanently she hopes to make the Sky rue the day they called it a wrap and dealt her?</p><p>“It’s just like any game,” she said. “I’m just looking forward to another opportunity to play basketball.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/06/sky-trade-angel-reese-to-atlanta-dream-for-2027-2028-draft-picks" >Sky trade Angel Reese to Dream for first-round draft picks in 2027, 2028</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/09/angel-reese-after-trade-i-just-feel-that-urge-right-now-that-im-going-to-win-again" target="_blank" >Angel Reese after trade: ‘I just feel that urge right now that I’m going to win again’</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Oh, and how happy is “happy” as she moves forward with one of the best — and most functional — organizations in the WNBA?</p><p>“I’ve shown all 32 teeth every day,” she said.</p><p>That’s 32 more than I saw the first time I attempted to interview her, early in her rookie season. That day, after a practice in Deerfield, she turned her back to me, said she didn’t trust me and accused me of aiming to twist her words, all of which was a bit strange given we’d never met. Then again, she didn’t owe me anything more than that considering the way basketball “fans” were abusing her online and some in the broadly defined “media” wanted to pigeonhole her as an enemy of Caitlin Clark.</p><p>Reese got through her time in Chicago, but it never seemed as enjoyable for her or rich with possibilities as it does in Atlanta, where the Dream are coming off a 30-14 season — 20 games better than the bumbling Sky were in the standings — and are right behind the Liberty, Aces and Fever among this year’s championship favorites.</p><p>An 87-78 exhibition win against the Sky didn’t mean much in the scheme of things, but Reese’s inability to wipe a smile off her face during warmups just might. Surrounded by serious talent — Rhyne Howard, Allisha Gray, Brionna Jones, Jordin Canada, Naz Hillmon — she has a chance not only to win but also to be the reason the Dream ascend to a new level.</p><p>Reese didn’t exactly look different in her first action with the Dream, missing some close-in shots and pounding the glass — her signatures with the Sky — but her level of intensity stood out, and that’s a good sign. Her enthusiasm with her new teammates did, too, including on the bench in the second half when, her night’s work done, she took on the role of lead cheerleader. It isn’t difficult at all to imagine these attributes being factors that send the Dream over the top.</p><p>“My first impression is she’s an All-Star-level player already, and I think she has some room to grow because she’s only been in the league a couple years,” Dream coach Karl Smesko said. “And I don’t think she’s satisfied with where she’s at. She wants to be one of the very best players in the league, and I think she has that potential.”</p><p>Reese will turn 24 four days before the Dream’s regular-season opener. It could be a tailor-made scenario for her on a team with such healthy vibes — completely unlike recent Sky squads — that Howard, Gray, Jones, Canada and Hillmon all re-signed as free agents despite a Wild West offseason of opportunity throughout the league.</p><p>Then again, Reese does have to work her way into an established culture, which can be harder than it sounds. It’s too soon for anyone to say she has already managed that.</p><p>But as a giddy Reese put it in a recent scrum with reporters in Atlanta, “It feels great. I mean, I can’t believe this is my life.”</p><p>If the Sky ever made her feel like that, we must have missed it.</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: Pete Kastanes, who spread nostalgia with Vanished Chicagoland social media posts, dies at 62
entries.summary: <p></p><p></p><p></p><p>For Pete Kastanes, nostalgia was like salt — properly sprinkled, it made everything better.</p><p>He liked to leaf through high school yearbooks and collect old comics. </p><p>His matchbook collection evoked images of neon bar signs.</p><p>His DVD collection leaned into old Disney capers featuring a young Hayley Mills. "That Darn Cat!" was one of his favorites.</p><p>As a kid, he wanted to go see things in Chicago that he'd heard about, like pizzerias and parks, and learn their histories. If a place really fascinated him, he'd go to the library and dig for information.</p><p>As an adult, the Internet fed his passion.</p><p>In 2011, Mr. Kastanes, inspired by a Facebook page that paid tribute to a shuttered Chicago business, he created one dedicated to the memory of a record store he frequented as a teen.</p><p>In 2012 he created a Facebook page called Chicago Extinct Businesses, later renamed <a class="Link" href="https://www.facebook.com/vanishedchicagoland/" target="_blank" >Vanished Chicagoland</a>. It celebrated things like defunct Chicago businesses and restaurants and invites people to share their memories and favorite forgotten spots.</p><p>He gained a loyal following of nearly 100,000 people on Facebook and hosted a podcast.</p><p>In recent years, he also shared details about his battle with cancer.</p><p>His last Facebook post on April 23 read, in part: "My cancer has spread. I don’t have time much left. As of Vanished Chicagoland the future will be a family decision. Thank you! For my making this fun. I love you all! And god bless!"</p><p>Mr. Kastanes died April 25. He was 62.</p><p>More than 3,000 people commented on his last Facebook post.</p><p>"Vanished Chicagoland wasn't polished, it wasn't some fancy production. It was just Pete sharing what he knew about a TV show or a restaurant or an amusement park, and other people sharing what they knew, and that created a community," said Eric Miller, development coordinator at the Chicago History Museum. "And he was very clear if someone wrote something negative or about politics he'd right away tell everyone 'I won't tolerate it.' And people loved that."</p><p>Mr. Kastanes was born Oct 4, 1963, and grew up in three different South Side neighborhoods: South Shore, Roseland and Ashburn.</p><p>His late father, Chris, was a longtime room service waiter at the downtown Hilton, and his mother, Zoe, was a homemaker and waitress. Both came to the United States from Greece.</p><p>"He's a good person, a good friend, a good son to me," said his mother. "He respected me, respected everybody. He's a good man."</p><p>Mr. Kastanes, a graduate of Bogan High School and DeVry University, lived in Oak Lawn, where he ran Vanished Chicago from his home computer during his free time.</p><p>He worked for American Express Travel, fielding phone calls and assisting customers with their miles and points when booking flights and hotels.</p><p>"It played to his strengths. Pete was friendly and outgoing," said his brother, John Kastanes.</p><p>Following an online groundswell calling for Mr. Kastanes to be recognized for his role in sharing the city's history, the Chicago History Museum created the Citizen History Award and presented it to him during a ceremony in February 2025.</p><p>"It was just a beautiful event, and this is hard for me to say," Miller said, taking a moment. "But a few weeks later he posted on Facebook that it was one of the best days of his life, and it made me feel really good."</p><p>Mr. Kastanes proudly displayed the plaque he received on a wall at his home, his brother said.</p><p>"He was not someone looking for recognition, he just loved it and people loved that about him," Miller said. "He was as Chicago as you can get. He exuded Chicago without pretense."</p><p>His family wasn't sure if Vanished Chicagoland would continue without Mr. Kastanes at the helm.</p><p>A <a class="Link" href="https://orricofuneral.com/pete-kastanes/" target="_blank" >wake will be held Thursday</a> from 3 to 8 p.m. at Palos-Gaidas Funeral Home in Palos Hills. A funeral will be held Friday at 10 a.m. at Saints Constantine & Helen Greek Orthodox Church in Palos Hills.</p>

entries.title: Horoscope for Thursday, April 30, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>Avoid shopping (except food and gas) and important decisions from 3:30 a.m. to 2:30 p.m. After that, the moon moves from Libra into Scorpio.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You might feel some tension building up before tomorrow's full moon. It will likely deal with financial matters, especially shared property, shared expenses, taxes, debt or something to do with inheritances and wills. By the weekend, the dust will settle.</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today there's a buildup of energy before tomorrow's full moon, the only one all year to take place directly opposite your sign. This means relations with partners, spouses and close friends are being tested. The best way to handle this is with patience. Patience is key.</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Each month, before the full moon, problems seem to intensify. After the full moon peaks, these same problems diminish and sometimes disappear. Tomorrow is a full moon. Today you might feel challenges with your health or dealing with co-workers and customers at work. Patience.</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You generally feel the difference in energy, sleep patterns and dealing with others during the full moon each month. Tomorrow is a full moon, which is why today you might feel tension dealing with your kids, romantic partners and groups. Be diplomatic.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> There's a full moon tomorrow, which means today you might feel some tension building up ahead of time. This particular full moon might create a tug-of-war between the demands of home and family versus the demands of your career and your external world. Cope as best you can.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Avoid controversial subjects today because they will be volatile due to the buildup of energy before tomorrow's full moon. Discussions about politics, religion and racial issues might have too much explosive intensity. Focus on something else. By the weekend, things are calmer.</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Financial arguments about your possessions, your property as well as how to share expenses or divide an inheritance might arise today because these issues and their energies are building before tomorrow's full moon. Postpone important discussions until the weekend.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Tomorrow the full moon is in your sign. This means there's an opposition between the sun in Taurus and the moon in Scorpio, which creates tension. Today you might feel this tension with those who are closest to you — partners, spouses and friends. By the weekend, all is well. (This is good.)</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You're keen to work and accomplish as much as possible right now. However, your energy might be blocked by others or by your own feelings because of a buildup before tomorrow's full moon. Pull back. Take it easy. Don't push the river.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Parents must be patient with their kids today because they can feel the energy building up before tomorrow's full moon. This can create tension with romantic relationships, relations with your kids, as well as relations with sports colleagues. Do your best to stay chill.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Relations with parents, bosses, the police and authority figures will be challenged today because a strong energy is building up before tomorrow's full moon. Don't go looking for trouble — it will find you. Be diplomatic. Seek escape in socializing with others.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a mildly accident-prone day for you, so pay attention to everything you say and do. The reason for this is a strong energy building up before tomorrow's full moon that can distract you or cause mix-ups in communication and transportation.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actress Kirsten Dunst (1982) shares your birthday today. You are calm, independent, and practical. You are also a perceptive professional and have an excellent sense of humor. This year you will reap the rewards of your past efforts; expect a promotion, opportunities, kudos and awards. You will be busy. Be ready to seize opportunities and act on them. </p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: One fatally burned in Northwest Side house fire
entries.summary: <p>A person was found dead at a Northwest Side home after a fire Thursday morning. </p><p>Chicago police and fire officials responded to the fire around 5:50 a.m. in the 5800 block of North Louise Avenue and removed a person who had suffered burns, police said. </p><p>The person, only identified as a male, was pronounced dead at the scene, police said. </p><p>The Chicago Fire Department was investigating the incident. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: UChicago students propose solutions to city's sports stadium conundrum
entries.summary: <p>From Springfield to Indianapolis, the debate over sports facilities is reaching a fever pitch as lawmakers vie for the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" >Bears</a> to build a new stadium in their backyard.</p><p>Similar conversations were had last week in Hyde Park as students at the University of Chicago’s Harris School of Public Policy gave their final pitches in the Harris Policy Innovation Challenge — tackling the issue of stadium policy in Chicago.</p><p>The annual contest, launched in 2023, pushes students to find solutions to some of Chicago’s most pressing public policy issues. Finalists are selected to present at a live pitch event, “Shark Tank”-style, and the winning team receives $10,000.</p><p>This year, students were asked what Chicago’s official policy for supporting professional sports teams and facilities should be.</p><p>Winners Charlie Schraw, Christina Tsai and Liz Williams presented judges with a three-pronged approach: create a new stadium securitization corporation, require a community benefits agreement for stadium developments and enforce sustainability standards.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-820000" name="image-820000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/68fc094/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2048x1149+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb9%2Fc9%2F1e3ead92425cae3285f8dc02e8db%2Fbmc05926-websize-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dd3ab2d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2048x1149+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb9%2Fc9%2F1e3ead92425cae3285f8dc02e8db%2Fbmc05926-websize-1.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/29007b3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2048x1149+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb9%2Fc9%2F1e3ead92425cae3285f8dc02e8db%2Fbmc05926-websize-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="University of Chicago students Liz Williams (left), Charlie Schraw and Christina Tsai hold their awards from the 2026 Harris Policy Innovation Challenge at the University of Chicago’s Harris School of Public Policy." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/29007b3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2048x1149+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb9%2Fc9%2F1e3ead92425cae3285f8dc02e8db%2Fbmc05926-websize-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/69f6fa8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2048x1149+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb9%2Fc9%2F1e3ead92425cae3285f8dc02e8db%2Fbmc05926-websize-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/29007b3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2048x1149+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb9%2Fc9%2F1e3ead92425cae3285f8dc02e8db%2Fbmc05926-websize-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>University of Chicago students Liz Williams (left), Charlie Schraw and Christina Tsai hold their awards from the 2026 Harris Policy Innovation Challenge at the University of Chicago’s Harris School of Public Policy.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Beking Media</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The stadium securitization corporation is modeled after Chicago’s Sales Tax Securitization Corp., which was established in 2017.</p><p>The stadium corporation would issue bonds strictly for developing the public infrastructure necessary for stadiums, according to the team’s presentation. It would serve as a bankruptcy-remote entity that separates stadium liabilities from the city’s general credit, according to the team.</p><p>Those funds would be lockboxed, meaning restricted to the corporation, resulting in lower interest rates on corporation debt, ultimately leading to declining stadium debt, Tsai said.</p><p></p><p>“The SSC provides a cost-saving framework for funding public infrastructure that's necessary for these stadium deals,” Tsai said during the event. “Having a plan in place allows the city to come to the negotiating table with discipline and leverage — not desperation — and this leverage is what allows the city to negotiate better deals for Chicagoans.”</p><p>Reforming the city’s community benefits agreement process was also important for the team, whose presentation was titled “Structuring the Deal.” The team argued their process, which would require a signed CBA before city bonds are issued, would prioritize Chicagoans in stadium deals.</p><p>Schraw said the team’s CBA model came from looking at projects in Chicago and beyond, including the Obama Presidential Center and the new Highmark Stadium for the Buffalo Bills. Those agreements require minority- and women-owned businesses to be involved in construction, in addition to hiring locally.</p><p>“Something that made our proposal really strong is that we had these considerations that oftentimes are seen as aspirational, in terms of community impact and sustainability, but within our proposal, these actually have teeth,” Williams said. “What really drove us to that is … this tension between the fans and the teams themselves. The fans love these teams, but what are they getting back from them? And we wanted to ensure that that relationship was truly a two-way street.”</p><p>Paired with the CBA are additional sustainability requirements that must be met for the stadium securitization corporation bonds to be issued. Stadiums must align with the city’s Climate Action Plan and produce annual public reports on their energy, water and waste for the duration of the lease. If the stadium is part of a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/04/22/bears-stadium-arlington-heights-property-tax-relief-infrastructure-northwest-indiana" >megadevelopment</a>, the requirements would include the entire project — not just the sports arena.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/04/24/bears-stadium-bill-jb-pritzker-illinois-senate" >Gov. JB Pritzker feels ‘need for speed’ — and changes — with Bears stadium bill</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Williams said while her team’s proposal may slow down negotiations or push teams away, it includes important factors for both the long-term use of stadiums and Chicago’s reputation as a sports city.</p><p>“This framework is for all leagues. When we imagine the Bears, we are also trying to imagine small women's sports leagues. We're also trying to imagine leagues that would grow over time. We're trying to imagine expansion, and that this would then apply to teams both big and small,” Williams said. “We really hope that this plan, these conditions and our enforcement mechanisms ensure that teams ... would actually take care of [fans] and pay them back. We would hope that a team like the Bears and the love that Chicago has for them would recognize that, and all of these conditions are just to improve the community, the durability and the accessibility of their stadium.”</p><p>Other proposals included instituting public referendums to trigger public stadium subsidies and a long-term restructuring of Soldier Field’s ownership structure and requiring sports teams to sell a small, minority equity stake to the city through a new Chicago Sports Authority.</p><p>Justin Marlowe, research professor at the University of Chicago and director of the Center for Municipal Finance, noticed a theme of rethinking stadium governance structure among the three finalists.</p><p>“I think for many people that's the issue: It's seen as this black box ... as something that’s sort of negotiated behind closed doors,” Marlowe said. “What this proposal does … is really center the community in that discussion. What [the winners] did in particular was deliberately blur the lines between community and fans, and essentially make the case to teams that, in fact, it's in your interest to be accommodating to the community and mindful of the community's needs because that's what sustainability for a fan base means in the long run.”</p>
entries.author: Abby Miller

entries.title: Trump's petty fights end up humiliating himself and the country
entries.summary: <p>When the history books write about Donald Trump, they’ll have a lot to say — little of it positive, I’d be willing to wager.</p><p>His presidencies have been marked by rank incompetence, unprecedented greed and self-dealing, naked corruption, ethical, legal and moral breaches and, as we repeatedly see, a rise in political division and anger. From impeachments to an insurrection to who-knows-what is still to come, the era of Trump has hardly been worthy of admiration.</p><p>But don’t tell that to his loyal supporters, for whom no one stands in higher esteem, despite Trump’s obvious shortcomings. Where we see an embarrassment, they see the fulfilment of a promise. Where we find horror, they find jubilation. We are truly living in two different Americas.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-8d0000" name="module-8d0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>It’s remarkable that Trump can so clearly be two opposing things depending on whom you ask, and that stark contrast is often revealed in moments where he’s waging war on perceived enemies.</p><p>This week, Trump’s Justice Department, under the leadership of Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche, announced it had indicted former FBI Director James Comey over an Instagram post in which Comey had photographed seashells on a beach to spell out “86 47.”</p><p>To be clear, “86” is common restaurant jargon to “nix” a menu item, and “47” refers to Trump. Blanche’s DOJ is claiming that this amounted to a threat of violence.</p><p>If that sounds silly, it’s because it is. But Trump’s got it out for Comey, and he tried this once before. This indictment, like the last one, isn’t likely to result in a prosecution.</p><p>But the indictment was met with predictable praise from MAGA loyalists, for whom Trump’s revenge campaigns are a titillating projection of his strength and a righteous use of presidential power.</p><p>For the rest of us, they are just another humiliation for Trump and the country — a weaponized and compromised DOJ that’s already seen one attorney general fired for failing to throw enough Trump opponents in prison, and a president who is pathologically consumed with old and irrelevant grudges.</p><p>Trump fans love it when he’s playing the bully and swinging at the people he’s told them to hate, from Jimmy Kimmel to Sen. Mark Kelly, D-Ariz., to New York Attorney General Letitia James.</p><p>For all of his efforts at projecting strength, Trump never looks weaker than in these moments, when he’s pursuing these personal vendettas — and losing.</p><p>Just in the past year, Trump’s DOJ has lost numerous high-profile cases it sought to use to appease the president’s bloodlust.</p><p>It failed to get an indictment against six Democratic lawmakers, including Kelly, over a video they released regarding illegal orders.</p><p>Grand juries rejected cases against protesters in Washington and elsewhere, including trying to charge a man with felony assault for throwing a sandwich at an officer.</p><p>Attempts to prosecute James, former CIA Director John Brennan, and Fed Chair Jerome Powell have thus far failed.</p><p>Trump has also failed to successfully sue a number of opponents, from Hillary Clinton to the Democratic National Committee, the New York Times to CNN.</p><p>Judges have overruled his attempts at silencing news outlets, blocking a Pentagon policy limiting reporter access, ordering the White House to lift restrictions on the Associated Press after Trump had banned the news agency for refusing to use the term “Gulf of America,” and blocking an executive order to cut funding for NPR and PBS.</p><p>Trump has lost so many of these petty fights, it’s hard to imagine why he keeps going back to the trough, only to suffer more humiliating losses.</p><p>And yet somehow, his fans don’t read these abject failures the same way the rest of us do. Where we see impotence and incompetence, they still see power and strength.</p><p>I can’t make it make sense, but I’m fairly confident that the history books, at least, will get it right.</p><p><i>S.E. Cupp is the host of “S.E. Cupp Unfiltered” on CNN.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-830000" name="module-830000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: S. E. Cupp

entries.title: High-tech crime-fighting, privacy rights square off in Supreme Court battle
entries.summary: <p>Seven years ago, police in Midlothian, Virginia, sought to identify a bank robber by asking Google to search the records of more than 500 million people who used the company's "location history" feature. That search identified 19 devices that were in or near the bank around the time of the robbery, which police winnowed down to three people, including Okello Chatrie, the man who was ultimately convicted of the crime.</p><p>Depending on your perspective, that use of a "geofence" warrant was either an unobjectionable example of smart police work or an outrageous invasion of privacy. On Monday, the Supreme Court <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2026/04/28/scotus-weighs-geofence-warrants-and-the-future-of-digital-privacy/" target="_blank" >weighed</a> the merits of those contending views in a <a class="Link" href="https://www.scotusblog.com/cases/chatrie-v-united-states/" target="_blank" >case</a> that illustrates the threat that two dubious doctrines pose to Fourth Amendment rights now that Americans routinely entrust huge volumes of personal information to tech companies that help them with myriad quotidian tasks.</p><p>In 1967, the Supreme Court <a class="Link" href="https://tile.loc.gov/storage-services/service/ll/usrep/usrep389/usrep389347/usrep389347.pdf" target="_blank" >said</a> the Fourth Amendment applies only when you have a "reasonable expectation of privacy." In subsequent cases that involved <a class="Link" href="https://tile.loc.gov/storage-services/service/ll/usrep/usrep425/usrep425435/usrep425435.pdf" target="_blank" >bank</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://tile.loc.gov/storage-services/service/ll/usrep/usrep442/usrep442735/usrep442735.pdf" target="_blank" >telephone</a> records, the court said you have no such expectation when you voluntarily share information with third parties for their own business use.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-f30000" name="module-f30000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>The problems with those principles were clear in a <a class="Link" href="https://www.supremecourt.gov/opinions/17pdf/16-402_h315.pdf" target="_blank" >2018 case</a> that addressed the FBI's tracking of a robbery suspect via location data collected by cellphone sites. Although the majority <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2018/06/22/scotus-rejects-warrantless-tracking-of-c/" target="_blank" >held</a> that such tracking generally requires a warrant, that conclusion was hard to reconcile with the third-party rule, as Justice Neil Gorsuch noted in his <a class="Link" href="https://www.supremecourt.gov/opinions/17pdf/16-402_h315.pdf#page=99" target="_blank" >dissent</a>.</p><p>Chatrie's case is broadly similar, except that the information used to identify him was <a class="Link" href="https://www.scotusblog.com/2026/04/digital-location-data-heads-back-to-the-supreme-court-/" target="_blank" >collected</a> by his own phone rather than cell sites. Although Google no longer keeps such data on its servers, many commonly used apps track the whereabouts of their users, and that information is often stored remotely.</p><p>In Chatrie's case, police obtained a warrant. But his lawyers <a class="Link" href="https://www.supremecourt.gov/DocketPDF/25/25-112/397074/20260223160717593_25-112%20-%20Opening%20Brief.pdf" target="_blank" >argue</a> that such orders sweep so broadly that they violate the Fourth Amendment, which says a warrant must be based on probable cause and must "particularly" describe "the place to be searched" and "the persons or things to be seized."</p><p>The Trump administration wants the Supreme Court to reject that argument. But it also <a class="Link" href="https://www.supremecourt.gov/DocketPDF/25/25-112/401871/20260325184404433_25-112bsUnitedStates.pdf" target="_blank" >maintains</a> that geofence inquiries do not require any sort of warrant because people who consent to location tracking have no reasonable expectation that the resulting information will be kept private.</p><p>If so, Chatrie's lawyer, Adam Unikowsky, warned during <a class="Link" href="https://www.supremecourt.gov/oral_arguments/argument_transcripts/2025/25-112_7k47.pdf" target="_blank" >oral argument</a> on Monday, the same logic would allow the government to search people's remotely stored emails, photos, calendars and documents at will. Several justices understandably viewed that prospect with alarm.</p><p>Although Deputy Solicitor General Eric Feigin assured the justices that his argument did not extend that far, those other examples likewise involve voluntary sharing of information with third parties, which supposedly makes the Fourth Amendment inapplicable. And even when the information is limited to location tracking, it can still reveal sensitive details of people's lives.</p><p>"The potential for abuse is breathtaking," Unikowsky <a class="Link" href="https://www.supremecourt.gov/DocketPDF/25/25-112/397074/20260223160717593_25-112%20-%20Opening%20Brief.pdf" target="_blank" >notes</a> in a Supreme Court brief. "The government need only draw a geofence around a church, a political rally or a gun shop, and it can compel a search of every user's records to learn who was there."</p><p>In addition to arguing that people reasonably view their location histories as private, Unikowsky takes a page from Gorsuch, who in 2018 questioned both that test and the third-party doctrine. Since the Fourth Amendment protects people's "papers" and "effects" against "unreasonable searches and seizures," Gorsuch suggested, the relevant question is whether the information sought by the government falls into those categories.</p><p>Gorsuch thought it was plausible to argue that people retain a property interest in their records even when they share them with others for specific purposes. That argument, which Unikowsky adopts, seems like a promising way to escape the danger posed by the third-party doctrine, which modern technology has magnified to an extent that would have dismayed the Fourth Amendment's framers.</p><p><i>Jacob Sullum is a senior editor at Reason magazine.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-9c0000" name="module-9c0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Jacob Sullum

entries.title: Dear Abby: Three's a crowd when unexpected visit drags on
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My partner recently allowed a friend to stay in our home without telling me in advance. My partner knows unannounced guests deeply affect my mental well-being and sense of safety. We had agreed I would be given at least two weeks' notice for any visitors. That did not happen because the guest was going through a difficult time.</p><p>While I have compassion and tried my best to be flexible, I also recognize that our home is not a healing center or a place to bring chaos. What I was told would be a short stay turned into an open-ended one, and my peace was severely disrupted. Shared spaces were left messy, personal belongings spread throughout the house, decor rearranged and boundaries ignored. The guest even began moving items from a van into our home!</p><p>When we all tried to calmly discuss a departure, the guest talked over me, attempted to analyze me and wanted us to work around their schedule rather than respect our home and needs. When they suggested staying in the van outside and coming inside only at certain times, I finally said, "I want our home back," and made it clear that this arrangement would not work for me.</p><p>What hurts me most is feeling unsupported by my partner. This situation has created tension, and I'm left wondering how we can repair the damage and restore peace in our relationship and, more important, our home. — <i>HOME INVASION IN COLORADO</i></p><p><b>DEAR HOME INVASION:</b> I understand why you justifiably felt invaded. You and your partner view your home differently. You need quiet and solitude, while your partner is more outgoing. The relationship is repairable if you can talk and reestablish your basic ground rules. Can you do that to the satisfaction of both of you? If this was a one-time thing, let it go. However, if it happens regularly, you may need to rethink the relationship and make different living arrangements.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I was in a relationship with my daughter's father for 11 years. From the beginning, he lied, manipulated and cheated. My family was aware of it. When I finally had had enough and left, my family decided to maintain a relationship with him (dinners, parties, camping, holidays). He is not a good person. I poured my heart out to let them know how badly what they were doing was hurting me.</p><p>That was six years ago, and they are all still going strong. My family and I have not spoken in five years. My heart is broken. It can never be repaired; the damage is done. I no longer want a relationship with them. My heart still hurts, and I don't know how to get beyond that pain. Can you help? — <i>SUFFERING IN NEW YORK</i></p><p><b>DEAR SUFFERING:</b> I am sorry for the pain you continue to experience because of your shattered family. I wish I could make it dissipate, but the person you need to consult (and by this, I mean talk to) would be a licensed psychotherapist. While your pain may not disappear overnight, it may lessen considerably if you do.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>What teens need to know about sex, drugs, AIDS and getting along with peers and parents is in "What Every Teen Should Know." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Teen Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: In Chinese money-laundering crackdown in Chicago, man admits helping send $3M to Mexican cartels
entries.summary: <p>A Chicago man has pleaded guilty in federal court to helping a money-laundering ring move about $3 million in illegal drug proceeds from Chicago to Mexico through China.</p><p>Last week, Weiliang Zheng admitted participating in international money-laundering. Under sentencing guidelines, he could get at least 70 months in prison. Di Xiong Luo, who is accused of being his partner, is awaiting trial.</p><p>From 2016 to 2018, according to prosecutors, Zheng and Luo picked up drug proceeds in parking lots in the Chicago area, counted the money and delivered the cash to a contact.</p><p>Undercover investigators posing as money-launderers arrested the two in April 2018, saying the pair gave the officers $95,000 in drug cash and the numbers of the Chinese bank accounts into which the proceeds were supposed to be transferred.</p><p>According to the indictment against Zheng and Luo, which was unsealed in 2023, their roles in the money-laundering scheme didn’t appear to be particularly lucrative. </p><p>For nearly two years of work as a money pickup man, Zheng, who lived in McKinley Park on the Southwest Side, made about $50,000, prosecutors said. Luo, who now lives in Oregon, was too poor to afford a plane ticket and hotel to come to Chicago for hearings, so a judge ordered the government to pay for it.</p><p>Their case was part of a larger effort by the U.S. government to crack down on Chinese money-laundering rings helping drug cartels move cash from Chicago to Mexico through China.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Seok Pheng Lim, an accused accomplice of Sui Yuet Kong, sent this photo to a Chinese counterpart in Mexico to show $340,000 in cash was delivered from a cartel courier to a money-laundering associate in New York in 2017. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>U.S. District Court trial exhibit</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In late 2015, the federal Drug Enforcement Administration began noticing <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2021/4/30/22408448/chinese-money-laundering-mexican-drug-cartel-mirror-swap-trade-based-money-laundering-xianbing-gan" target="_blank" >Chinese nationals were laundering money</a> for the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/el-chapo" >cartels</a>. Chinese operatives were replacing Mexican money launderers, who were slower at their work and demanded higher fees than their Chinese counterparts.</p><p>In 2019, a federal Homeland Security Investigations case in Chicago led to charges against two Chinese men living in Mexico. They were accused of running a ring that transferred tens of millions of dollars in illegal U.S. drug money to Mexican cartels via China. </p><p>Auhorities said the money came from drug sales in Chicago, New York, Los Angeles and other areas in 2017 and 2018 and that the operation used “mirror swaps,” in which U.S.-based Chinese brokers transferred Chinese currency from bank accounts they held in China to Mexico-based money-launderers’ accounts in China.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-520000" name="image-520000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fb83af9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1622x910+0+173/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F86%2Fe1%2F33897908a47d8723890e38c0c326%2Fscreen-shot-2021-04-29-at-5-59-55-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/47d4576/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1622x910+0+173/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F86%2Fe1%2F33897908a47d8723890e38c0c326%2Fscreen-shot-2021-04-29-at-5-59-55-pm.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fef6670/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1622x910+0+173/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F86%2Fe1%2F33897908a47d8723890e38c0c326%2Fscreen-shot-2021-04-29-at-5-59-55-pm.png" alt="This chart, introduced in court, shows how cash from Mexican drug cartels was moved to Chinese money-laundering organizations. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fef6670/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1622x910+0+173/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F86%2Fe1%2F33897908a47d8723890e38c0c326%2Fscreen-shot-2021-04-29-at-5-59-55-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2139fde/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1622x910+0+173/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F86%2Fe1%2F33897908a47d8723890e38c0c326%2Fscreen-shot-2021-04-29-at-5-59-55-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fef6670/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1622x910+0+173/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F86%2Fe1%2F33897908a47d8723890e38c0c326%2Fscreen-shot-2021-04-29-at-5-59-55-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>This chart, introduced in court, shows how cash from Mexican drug cartels was moved to Chinese money-laundering organizations. Xianbing Gan, a money-laundering broker in Mexico, had a bank account in China. An associate in the United States collected cash from cartel drug sales, removed an equal amount from a bank account in China and deposited it in Gan’s Chinese account to get around the U.S. banking system and try to avoid detection, according to prosecutors, in what’s called a mirror swap. A second swap was then done in Mexico to complete the money-laundering and try to make the illegal drug proceeds look like a legitimate business transaction.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>U.S District Court trial exhibit</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The amount of Chinese currency they transferred was equal to the amount of U.S. drug currency that the U.S.-based brokers kept. </p><p>The scheme allowed the ring to bypass the U.S. banking system and help cartels avoid huge losses of cash to law enforcement agencies stopping vehicles transporting drug proceeds to Mexico, officials said.</p><p>One convicted ringleader, Mexico-based money-launderer Xianbing Gan, was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2020/3/3/21162642/xianbing-gan-convicted-mexican-drug-money-laundering-china-chicago" target="_blank" >sentenced to 14 years in prison</a>. During his 2020 federal trial in Chicago, Gan said he was a seafood merchant and not knowingly involved in the drug business.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div
