2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/74e74dc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1446x812+0+53/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8f%2Fa9%2Ff9c7d24542e6be2ebc6fbd1f051f%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-29-at-4-03-41-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Haiping Pan." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/74e74dc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1446x812+0+53/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8f%2Fa9%2Ff9c7d24542e6be2ebc6fbd1f051f%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-29-at-4-03-41-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/181be40/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1446x812+0+53/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8f%2Fa9%2Ff9c7d24542e6be2ebc6fbd1f051f%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-29-at-4-03-41-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/74e74dc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1446x812+0+53/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8f%2Fa9%2Ff9c7d24542e6be2ebc6fbd1f051f%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-29-at-4-03-41-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Haiping Pan was handed over to U.S. agents in 2022 at the Mexico City International Airport.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Mexico attorney general’s office (FGR).</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In 2023, Gan’s partner Haiping Pan <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2021/6/11/22529643/chinese-money-laundering-mexican-drug-cartels-sui-yuet-kong" target="_blank" >pleaded guilty in federal court</a> in Chicago to laundering $62 million in drug money. He was sentenced to 10 years in prison. Prosecutors said he was among the highest-level money-launderers convicted in the United States.</p><p>Pan moved from China to Guadalajara, Mexico, in 2004. Before he got into money-laundering, he operated a business that imported inexpensive products, such as toys, his lawyer said in a court filing. He made about $250,000 for his money-laundering work, prosecutors said.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2021/6/11/22529643/chinese-money-laundering-mexican-drug-cartels-sui-yuet-kong" target="_blank" >Chinese immigrant in money-laundering ring: Didn’t know $24 million I picked up was from cartels</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2021/4/30/22408448/chinese-money-laundering-mexican-drug-cartel-mirror-swap-trade-based-money-laundering-xianbing-gan" target="_blank" >Chinese money-laundering rings in Chicago, New York cleaning Mexican drug cartel cash</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/27/ivy-hall-cocaine-money-launder-david-berger-pritzker-lashawn-ford-weed-cannabis" target="_blank" >Convicted cocaine cash launderer is a key figure in legal Illinois cannabis empire</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>

entries.title: Obama Presidential Center Museum tickets go on sale May 6. Here’s what to know
entries.summary: <p>A ticket to the Obama Presidential Center is shaping up to be one of the summer’s hottest commodities. On May 6, when ticket sales open, thousands will likely flood the <a class="Link" href="http://obama.org" target="_blank" ><u>obama.org</u></a> website to secure admission to the museum on the 19.3-acre site on Chicago’s South Side.</p><p>Here’s what you need to know about planning your trip to the campus.</p><h3>When does the Obama Presidential Center open? </h3><p>The Obama Presidential Center will host an invitation-only grand opening ceremony on June 18 that can be livestreamed at <a class="Link" href="http://obama.org" target="_blank" ><u>obama.org</u></a> and on the Obama Foundation social media accounts. The museum will open to the public on June 19, from 10 a.m. to 5 p.m. Located at 6001 S. Stony Island Ave. in Jackson Park, the Obama Presidential Center campus will be open 6 a.m. to 9 p.m. Monday through Friday, while the museum will be open 1 p.m. to 8 p.m. Monday and 10 a.m. to 5 p.m. Tuesday through Sunday.</p><h3>When do OPC tickets go on sale?</h3><p>Tickets will go on sale May 6 at <a class="Link" href="http://obama.org" target="_blank" ><u>obama.org</u></a>. The OPC will announce the exact time that sales will open in the near future. Museum entry is time-ticketed, and guests will be able to select the date and time they’d like to visit, though November 30. Tickets for future dates will be made available at a later time. Buying your tickets in advance is recommended; but, a spokesperson says tickets may be available on the day of at the front desk, if tickets are not already sold out. The online ticket inventory is the same as the in-person ticket inventory. Guests are encouraged to arrive 10 minutes ahead of scheduled entry time. Tickets are $30 for adults and $23 for children 3 to 11. Children under 2 may enter for free. For Illinois residents, tickets are $26 for adults and $15 for children 3 to 11.</p><h3>Are OPC tickets for opening day already sold out? </h3><p>Donors were offered early access to tickets in April, so, yes, some opening-day tickets are already gone, and OPC anticipates that the remainder will sell out quickly. The center will assess how many daily visitors can be accommodated during a soft opening period.</p><h3></h3><h3>Holy smokes, $30 a ticket seems expensive! </h3><p>General admission for the Obama Presidential Center is comparable to some of the top museums in the city, including the Art Institute of Chicago, the Field Museum of Natural History and the Griffin Museum of Science and Industry. OPC offers a $4 discount for Illinois residents; kids get in for $15, compared to $23 for out-of-staters. On Tuesdays, Illinois residents enter for free.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-350000" name="html-embed-module-350000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-chart" data-src="visualisation/28685904"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/28685904/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="chart visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p></p><h3>What’s included in admission?</h3><p>At the museum, visitors will have access to multiple floors of exhibits and attractions, including an exploration of election night 2008, a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/architecture-design/2026/04/04/obama-presidential-center-replica-oval-office-michael-smith" ><u>replica of President Obama’s Oval Office</u></a> and a panoramic view of the city from the Sky Room. A 5,000-square-foot rotating exhibition space will be used for special exhibitions in the future, but visitors can enjoy free access to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/art/2026/03/19/obama-presidential-center-contemporary-art-collection-barack-michelle-virginia-shore" ><u>art installations</u></a> throughout the campus during operating hours. Guests are encouraged to allot at least two hours for their visit. Visitors can grab quick bites at the cafe on the lower level of the museum, or dine at the restaurant led by <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/food-drink/2026/01/21/chef-cliff-rome-obama-presidential-center" ><u>Chicago chef Cliff Rome</u></a>.</p><p>For an extra $75 per person, you can get a 90-minute tour of the Obama Presidential Center campus, which includes stops at the Home Court athletic center and the Presidential Suite, a 3,000-square-foot space designed with input from the Obamas, which is typically closed to the public. Access to the rest of the museum is included.</p><p>While tickets are required for the museum, visitors can access most areas of the Obama Presidential Center’s campus for free beginning June 19. This includes a playground, gardens, walking trails and a branch of the Chicago Public Library.</p><p>Details about special programs at the OPC are forthcoming, but registration will be separate from museum tickets. The public can sign up to receive updates on ticket sales, events and other information at <a class="Link" href="http://obama.org/newsletters" target="_blank" ><u>obama.org/newsletters</u></a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-350000" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Home Court at the Obama Presidential Center will be available to the community only through special programming. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of The Obama Foundation</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>What’s the deal with the basketball court? How can I play on it? </h3><p>Visitors can explore Home Court for free daily, from noon to 8 p.m. However, community members will only be permitted to play on the court through sports and well-being programs. Details about those opportunities will be released in the future.</p><h3>Can OPC tickets be resold or transferred?</h3><p>No. Tickets to the Obama Presidential Center Museum may not be returned, transferred or sold. The tickets are not name-specific and I.D. is not required at the door (except for Illinois-resident tickets), but the OPC has technical safeguards to ensure compliance. However, visits may be rescheduled no later than 24 hours in advance.</p>
entries.author: Erica Thompson

entries.title: Oak Brook resident helps immigrant families still living in fear after Operation Midway Blitz
entries.summary: <p>When videos emerged of immigrant men running from federal immigration agents last fall, Patricia Gamboa was flooded with memories of fleeing agents herself as a little girl.</p><p>Gamboa was 6 when she and her family crossed the border from Tijuana into California in the mid-1980s, guided by a “coyote” who warned them to run if they saw “La Migra.” She remembers immigration agents showing up and hiding from them in a ditch, where she fell asleep. Her family continued their journey and eventually found their way to Chicago.</p><p>Gamboa, 49, is now a U.S. citizen living in Oak Brook. She was shocked to see immigrants running for their lives amid Operation Midway Blitz, President Trump's aggressive deportation campaign, last October.<b> </b>She felt the best way to help families afraid to leave their homes was by delivering food and supplies to their doors. The need was so great that she felt inspired to start a nonprofit that would continue to support them.</p><p>With her younger self in mind, she launched Mano a Mano Unidos, a nonprofit dedicated to supporting immigrants and families in need.</p><p>“That experience of fear, but also of success because we didn't get captured, that's why I wanted to help people,” Gamboa said. “I wanted to help because I know the fear of what it is to be undocumented.”</p><p>The organization, now headquartered in her office in suburban Berwyn, has grown to about 40 volunteers who donate their time to help families with anything from grocery runs to rides to doctor appointments. They also accept donations to help families pay medical bills, rent or moving fees.</p><p>Mano a Mano is more than just a pantry or delivery service, she said. It’s lending a helping hand.</p><h3>‘They could trust me’</h3><p>During the blitz, hundreds of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol agents flooded the area, stopping people of color on their way to work, raiding homes and making thousands of arrests. The immigration enforcement campaign left hundreds of immigrant families afraid to leave their homes for fear of deportation.</p><p>“People were so afraid of leaving that they were starving inside their homes,” Gamboa said.</p><p>So she began asking friends on social media to donate food and personally delivered it to families in need. Protecting those families was always on her mind. She kept track of deliveries on sheets of paper, but with limited information to protect their identities. She said she never even met some of the families in person, choosing to limit contact to whatever made them feel most comfortable.</p><p>Eventually word began to spread among the immigrant communities across the South and Southwest sides of the city and in suburbs like Berwyn and Cicero. Gamboa said she was making deliveries to 40 to 50 homes a day at the height of the immigration campaign, sometimes working until the early morning.</p><p>“Families started letting other families know they could trust me,” said Gamboa.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-960000" name="image-960000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/487cfb3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+159/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcb%2Fa6%2F9ecd8b374e6db502122e8b0e5c95%2Fmanoamano-260427-10.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/60a15ca/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+159/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcb%2Fa6%2F9ecd8b374e6db502122e8b0e5c95%2Fmanoamano-260427-10.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b0fa068/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+159/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcb%2Fa6%2F9ecd8b374e6db502122e8b0e5c95%2Fmanoamano-260427-10.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Opening of Mano a Mano Unidos, a new organization helping immigrant families." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b0fa068/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+159/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcb%2Fa6%2F9ecd8b374e6db502122e8b0e5c95%2Fmanoamano-260427-10.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8776d4c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+159/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcb%2Fa6%2F9ecd8b374e6db502122e8b0e5c95%2Fmanoamano-260427-10.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b0fa068/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+159/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcb%2Fa6%2F9ecd8b374e6db502122e8b0e5c95%2Fmanoamano-260427-10.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Patricia Gamboa has turned her office in Berwyn into the headquarters for Mano a Mano Unidos.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Victor Hilitski/For the Chicago Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>At first, Gamboa managed the deliveries herself. Two months later, she began recruiting volunteers to deliver groceries to families. In December, Mano a Mano Unidos received its 501(c)(3) status and became an officially recognized nonprofit organization. (Gamboa's organization is not affiliated with the similary named Mano a Mano, a Round Lake-based nonprofit that also supports immigrant families.)</p><p>Gamboa has helped more than 800 families since October and still makes regular deliveries to over 100, she said. The organization makes about 20 deliveries a day.</p><p>She even made it her mission to help immigrants in Minneapolis during Operation Metro Surge, where 3,000 federal agents flooded neighborhoods, leading to arrests, mass protests and the shooting deaths of two U.S. citizens, Renee Good and Alex Pretti, who were killed by federal officers in separate incidents.</p><p>“One day I woke up and I said, ‘I have to go over there,’” said Gamboa. “I didn't know people. People didn’t know me, but I had to bring help to them.”</p><p>Gamboa said she loaded up her car with food and supplies and made the journey of six-plus hours by herself. She connected with a community group there and said her car was emptied out by the end of the day.</p><p>She made the trip two more times after that.</p><h3>A family on lockdown</h3><p>Rolando Soto and his family were among the many whom Gamboa helped.</p><p>Soto, who works as a handyman and taco vendor, stayed home for four months last fall out of fear that federal immigration agents would detain him. He and his wife stopped working, and his daughter switched to virtual learning after losing her ride to school.</p><p>The nights felt long, he said, but the days felt even longer. The farthest he would venture out was into his back yard.</p><p>“I’ve never been in trouble, and I don’t have a criminal record, but simply being undocumented was the problem,” said Soto. “ICE was grabbing people just for looking Hispanic."</p><p>Soto, of Cicero, said he used his savings to pay for food deliveries and bills, but after coming across one of Gamboa’s social media posts, he reached out for help. Gamboa answered that same day and within hours had dropped off food at his and his in-laws’ home.</p><p>During those months, Gamboa regularly checked in and offered help with whatever Soto and his family needed.</p><p>“She helps out of the kindness of her heart, not because she expects anything in return,” Soto said.</p><p>He said he still hesitates to leave home, checking his security cameras before stepping outside and remaining wary of unfamiliar vehicles in the neighborhood.</p><p>“It’s hard not to panic and worry,” he said. </p><p>But now that he's back to work, Soto no longer needs help from the organization. Instead, he's offered his time to help Gamboa as a way of giving back.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-6f0000" name="image-6f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/78b7928/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fea%2F1b%2F6e1872db4ca38ec1106f1501d79a%2Fmanoamano-260427-4.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c67df24/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fea%2F1b%2F6e1872db4ca38ec1106f1501d79a%2Fmanoamano-260427-4.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7524c93/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fea%2F1b%2F6e1872db4ca38ec1106f1501d79a%2Fmanoamano-260427-4.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Opening of Mano a Mano Unidos, a new organization helping immigrant families." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7524c93/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fea%2F1b%2F6e1872db4ca38ec1106f1501d79a%2Fmanoamano-260427-4.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c0d773/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fea%2F1b%2F6e1872db4ca38ec1106f1501d79a%2Fmanoamano-260427-4.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7524c93/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fea%2F1b%2F6e1872db4ca38ec1106f1501d79a%2Fmanoamano-260427-4.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Executive director Patricia Gamboa, left, organizes donations during the grand opening of Mano a Mano Unidos in a new location Saturday in Berwyn.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Victor Hilitski/For the Chicago Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Hoping to expand</h3><p>Though immigration enforcement appears to have slowed down in the Chicago area, the need hasn’t.</p><p>Gamboa, who works in real estate, transformed her office in Berwyn into Mano a Mano’s base of operations. Bookshelves that once held office materials are now stocked with dry goods, diapers, clothes and shoes. The space has become a community hub where people can drop off donations and where families willing to leave their homes can pick up essential items.</p><p>While some immigrants are still afraid to stay out for long, many have returned to work and are trying to recover financially after spending months unable to leave their homes or earn an income. Gamboa said families with a relative who was detained or deported are now burdened by debt from legal fees.</p><p>Gamboa’s help has also now extended to single mothers affected by cuts to the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program. She said she now helps seniors and several mothers with young children with baby formula, diapers, wipes and other items they may need.</p><p>“The rules changed, and some moms no longer qualify for the same amount of money that they were getting, and they're falling behind," Gamboa said. “They don't have enough for their diapers or formula to feed their babies.”</p><p>About 400,000 Illinois residents could lose SNAP benefits, formerly known as food stamps, by May 1 under new work requirements, state officials <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/01/21/new-snap-work-requirements-cook-county-warn-health-consequences-cut-off-congress-donald-trump-food-stamps" target="_blank" >warned earlier this year</a>. The changes are part of President Donald Trump’s tax and spending plan approved by Congress last summer. Nearly 2 million Illinois residents are enrolled in the program.</p><p>Gamboa said she hopes Mano a Mano Unidos will continue to grow and eventually expand to other cities.</p><p>“People should feel safe in their homes and no one should have to go hungry,” she said.</p>
entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: What does it cost to open a Chicago indie restaurant?
entries.summary: <p>They say necessity is the mother of invention. When Jeremy Leven, chef and partner behind the forthcoming West Town pintxos bar Gilda was denied a bank loan, the team had to get creative.</p><p>Beverage director Ines de Haro devised a loyalty program built on community investment. Customers who donate any amount earn discounts and perks once the 44-seater opens on June 1.</p><div class="Enhancement FormEnhancement" data-align-center> <div id="Form-newsletter-wrapper" (“comfortable”), but the card is a key example of how the team is trying to close a funding gap. So far, the effort appears to be paying off: Having raised more than 82% of its $20,000 goal, the restaurant’s owners hope the Compañero (“buddy” in Spanish) card builds early regulars and encourages consumer appreciation for small, independent spots.</p><p>Their experiment will be closely watched in an era that increasingly favors the corporate-backed and deep-pocketed and in a year where several local independent restaurateurs have closed, citing <a class="Link" href="https://blockclubchicago.org/2026/04/20/after-viral-lifeline-from-tiktoker-keith-lee-amici-is-closing/" target="_blank" ><u>shaky industry economics</u></a>.</p><p>“If you have a ton of money, you can throw money at your problems,” said Leven, who’s opening Gilda alongside fellow chef-partners Rafael Esparza and and Anthony Baier. “If you don't, you have to be strategic. Maybe we’re delusional or old school, but we’re betting that a community-based model and way of thinking can still succeed in the restaurant industry.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Leven (mfk., Mama Delia) and Esparza (Momotaro, Kimski, FAFO) have collectively spent almost 40 years working in Chicago kitchens. The pair joined forces this winter with Baier and de Haro on the modern, transcultural pintxos bar, which Leven has been road-testing as a pop-up series. </p><p>They secured a lease on a 1,600-sq.-ft. storefront in January, in the former Chi-Latin Restaurant space, before they even started fundraising. With help from their families, they quickly scraped together about $70,000 — enough to cover city licenses, a modest buildout and incidentals to get the doors open.</p><p>But opening requires approximately double that, including funds for kitchen and bar inventory, staff and repayment of a few small existing loans, and a roughly four-month operating cushion. It helps that food suppliers who’ve known the chefs for years are doing what they can to work on spec for the first month.</p><p>Key to their plans was a Small Business Administration (SBA) loan, a common path for independent restaurants. In <a class="Link" href="https://www.lendingtree.com/business/sba-loans-study/" target="_blank" ><u>2024</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.monitordaily.com/originator/the-current-landscape-of-sba-lending-top-lenders-and-key-industries/" target="_blank" ><u>2023</u></a>, restaurants and hotels received the highest share of SBA loans of any industry. At the same time, small business loan and line of credit rejection rates remain high since the pandemic, according to reporting by <a class="Link" href="https://www.forbes.com/sites/zennonkapron/2026/01/25/why-banks-are-rejecting-too-many-small-business-loans/" target="_blank" ><u>Forbes</u></a>.</p><p>Compared to 2019, significantly fewer companies in 2024 were fully approved for financing: 51% compared to 62%, per the <a class="Link" href="https://www.fedsmallbusiness.org/reports/survey/2025/2025-report-on-employer-firms" target="_blank" ><u>Small Business Credit Survey</u></a>. In particular, SBA loan or line of credit applicants experienced a 45% denial rate in 2024 — more than double the 21% figure across all loan types.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d20000" name="image-d20000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/08694ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0c%2Fd9%2F41a34a794cec9558b42b5418260b%2Fgilda-cost-2605060125.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b3b9cf9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0c%2Fd9%2F41a34a794cec9558b42b5418260b%2Fgilda-cost-2605060125.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dccf34f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0c%2Fd9%2F41a34a794cec9558b42b5418260b%2Fgilda-cost-2605060125.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Gilda on Chicago Ave. in West Town. class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Leven and Esparza secured a lease on a 1,600-sq.-ft. storefront in January in the former Chi-Latin Restaurant space.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Back in December before he’d even found a space, Leven recruited a financial strategist to help him with the notoriously tedious SBA loan application process, amassing income statements, tax returns, loan documents and licenses, squaring away taxes and paying off credit cards.</p><p>“I'll be honest, we’re not the corporate America, buttoned-up type of guys,” Leven said. “There are definitely some cultural gaps between the way we’ve lived our lives and run restaurants and the way a financial institution and the government would like to see a secured [borrower].”</p><p>They felt cautiously optimistic once the lender preapproved them without raising concerns. The lease negotiations inspired more confidence. The landlord, who knew the chefs and believed in the project, lowered their base rent and capped their monthly occupancy costs at $5,000, comparatively low for the neighborhood, Leven said. They wouldn’t be charged rent until April, giving them a bit of breathing room.</p><p>So they hired a designer and general contractor to start work on a modest, mostly DIY buildout. When the exhaust hood broke down and needed a new motor, friends connected them to contact at Granger Mechanical Co., where they secured the part at cost and installed it themselves.</p><p>They reached the underwriting phase in March and that’s when things took a turn. Their lender said they were denied pretty quickly without a chance to appeal. That day, Leven opened as many credit cards as he was allowed (four, if you're wondering), and they went back to the drawing board on how to raise at least another $60,000 in 10 or so weeks.</p><p>De Haro, working out the math on a guest check, floated an idea for a buy-in program based on a similar approach by her friend Sam Nelis. He owns <a class="Link" href="https://www.specs-cafe-bar.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Specs</u></a>, an indie all-day cafe, bar and retail shop in Winooski, Vermont. As Nelis bootstrapped the space toward its final form between 2023 and 2025, he fundraised partly through offering <a class="Link" href="https://www.specs-cafe-bar.com/upcoming-events/sneakpeek#:~:text=Purchase%20a%20gift%20card%20and,retail%20shop%20or%20cocktail%20bar!" target="_blank" ><u>Community Fun-Ding</u></a> gift cards, which dangled a percentage extra that increased based on folks’ contributions.</p><p>“I was thinking of something where guests could buy in and keep using it over time until the amount they purchased is expended,” said de Haro. “We wanted to work a hospitality sense into it, where we keep folks coming back because there’s a variety of experiences they’re buying into.”</p><div class="Enhancement" #F4F4F4; border-left: 8px solid transparent; transform: translateY(-4px) translateX(8px);"></div></div></div> <div style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; flex-grow: 1; justify-content: center; margin-bottom: 24px;"> <div style=" background-color: #F4F4F4; border-radius: 4px; flex-grow: 0; height: 14px; margin-bottom: 6px; width: 224px;"></div> <div style=" background-color: #F4F4F4; border-radius: 4px; flex-grow: 0; height: 14px; width: 144px;"></div></div></a></div></blockquote> <script async src="//platform.instagram.com/en_US/embeds.js"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Customers who donate any amount (the suggested minimum is $250) in turn receive a Compañero card, which is good for a percentage off their bill every time they dine at Gilda until their full contribution is “paid back” (up to $499 gets them 8% back, $500 to $999, 10% and $1,000, 12%). The bigger the donation, the more Gilda will “give back,” not just in terms of discounts but in preferential treatment for reservations and first dibs on goings on at the restaurant.</p><p>As of April 28, about 30 donors had chipped in. Donations stalled a little following an initial post-announcement flurry, Leven said. But he’s optimistic, noting that at Gilda’s latest <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DWrTiRgjnMW/?img_index=3" target="_blank" ><u>Monday Night Foodball popup</u></a> in mid-April, they sold out within an hour and a half.</p><p>Not that they’d turn down more investment or equity injections, which they’re discussing with a handful of small and larger potential backers. In the meantime, they’re betting on their community and themselves to see it through.</p><p>“It’s always been tough to open a restaurant since they invented them,” Esparza said. “It’s an unstable, handle-with-caution kind of business. Is s— more expensive now? Absolutely. What matters to all of us is how bad do you want it? If we’re telling people to believe in ourselves, we have to believe in ourselves all the way. We’re proving it can be done this way.”</p>
entries.author: Maggie Hennessy | WBEZ

entries.title: Illinois advocacy groups say DOJ 'quietly gutting' legal aid services for low-income immigrants
entries.summary: <p>Thirteen Illinois-based nonprofits are decrying what they say is the quiet dismantling of a longtime federal program that helps provide legal aid to low-income immigrants.</p><p>For more than 60 years, the Department of Justice has operated the Recognition and Accreditation Program — a program that lets non-attorneys provide legal services and has made affordable representation available to families who cannot afford a private attorney.</p><p>The senior DOJ attorneys who ran the program were abruptly reassigned to work as entry-level law clerks, according to The Resurrection Project and a dozen other partner agencies.</p><p>“I think the federal government has been attacking immigrants, our neighbors, our teachers, our workers, from the beginning. Limiting people's ability to live a full life, to participate in their communities, by stopping them from even having access to a lawyer when they're in court, is just one more way that the federal administration is doing that,” said Tovia Siegel, director of organizing and leadership for Immigrant Justice at The Resurrection Project of Chicago.</p><p>The group said there were no advance warnings to the more than 900 nonprofit organizations and 2,600 accredited representatives nationwide who participate in the program.</p><p>Siegel said there is already a massive need for representation while there is also a lack of due process for immigrants who are applying for benefits while also fighting deportation.</p><p>“What this program does is it expands access … to high quality, trusted legal representation through nonprofits, to affordable, low or no-cost representation, a really essential service that immigrants need,” Siegel said. “The dismantling of this program is just one more insidious way that the Trump administration has undercut the ability for people to have due process when they face their day in court, when they're facing potential family separation or being sent to a country that they do not believe they can be safe in, or sometimes that they've never even lived in.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8ecd045/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0c%2F8e%2F4f20063542e5992fc5bafe8b7329%2F20250710-icepathways-001.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Chicago Immigration Court located at 55 E Monroe St., July 10, 2025.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Mendy Kong/WBEZ/Mendy Kong/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Liza Gutierrez, the director of immigrant programs at Partners for Our Communities in Palatine, said it’s a tremendous loss not having the program.</p><p>“We've been fighting for equity in getting representation to the most needy in our communities. When it comes to obtaining immigration reliever representation, there's just not enough and people cannot afford to go to your traditional attorney,” said Gutierrez, who is herself a DOJ accredited representative.</p><p>Without the program, Gutierrez fears low-income immigrants desperate to obtain legal status may get taken advantage of by others.</p><p>“We see people getting charged $10,000 to $20,000 for filing an asylum case, which is only like a $100 yearly fee,” Gutierrez said. “This means that now folks that are lower on the income scale will not be able to have that access to representation. We know that that representation is what's going to make a case winnable. When you show up to court without representation, you're most likely not going to win the case.”</p><p>A spokesman for the Department of Justice did not provide a response to questions about the program.</p><p>Other Illinois groups that have signed a letter in support of the program are: Partners for Our Communities (POC); Hanul Family Alliance; Binational Institute of Human Development; Spanish Community Center; Latino Policy Forum; AKWAABA QC; La Casa Norte; Chicago Workers Collaborative; FEDECMI / Casa Michoacan; Instituto Del Progreso Latino; United African Organization (UAO); and Erie Neighborhood House</p><p>The groups are asking DOJ to return the senior attorneys back to their roles with the Recognition and Accreditation program. They are also calling on Congress to exercise its oversight authority and demand a full public accounting for the changes to the program.</p>

entries.title: A soulful night at the Uptown Poetry Slam with Chicago’s new poet laureate, Mayda Del Valle
entries.summary: <p>The iconic UptownPoetry Slam is practically etched in stone on the Green Mill calendar. But April’s edition featured a particularly special performance: Chicago’s new poet laureate, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/books/2026/01/07/mayda-alexandra-del-valle-named-chicago-poet-laureate" target="_blank" ><u>Mayda Del Valle,</u></a> a South Side native, was slated to appear on the bill.</p><p>Inside the same bar where Al Capone’s legend looms large, audience members buzzed about the poet laureate’s expected appearance.</p><div class="Enhancement FormEnhancement" target="_blank" >Privacy Policy</a></p> </div> </form> </bsp-form> </div> </div><p>If you’re a poetry enthusiast and live in Chicago, you’ve almost assuredly been to the Slam: It’s a place where writers cut their teeth, find community, brave an audience for the first time, or even get married. Nestled in the back corner behind a still functioning juke box sat David Rubin, who met and married his wife in the 90’s at the Slam.</p><p>“I’m really looking forward to this, I haven’t seen this new poet laureate yet,” said Rubin. He hushed as Slam founder and emcee Marc Smith took the stage to invite up Del Valle.</p><p>“She got her chops at the Nuyorican Poet’s Cafe, but we won’t hold that against her," Smith joked about her getting her start in New York. “ Like so many of the performance poets, they have done so much over these last 15, 20 years. They're just sought after because they not only know the art of writing great poetry, they know the art of performing it in front of an audience. And that's what the Slam is all about. Performance and poetry.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-880000" name="image-880000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fc29b36/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2714x1523+0+56/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2c%2Ff3%2Fe102409043a5841373be19162968%2Fmarc-smith.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f23b76e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2714x1523+0+56/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2c%2Ff3%2Fe102409043a5841373be19162968%2Fmarc-smith.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/84eae1c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2714x1523+0+56/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2c%2Ff3%2Fe102409043a5841373be19162968%2Fmarc-smith.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Marc Smith organizer of the "Uptown poetry slam". data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f4ccc15/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9c%2Ffa%2F0c8bd12141fcab81b003370c7ad7%2Fpoet-laureate-slam-260430-016.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Del Valle’s poems traversed a range of themes: falling in love, the lake and landscape, her family, all with a deep tie to Chicago and place.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Del Valle took the stage, said it had only been four months since was named to the laureate post and launched into her verses. “ I'm gonna read some poems about being from Chicago,” she said, before reading the first of eight works.</p><p>The first poem spoke to the experience of Puerto Rican immigration, with the repetition of every line beginning with, “We came.” The crowd cheered as she ended and then introduced her next poem about language with the classic slam-style rhythmic expression. The poem expressed coming back home:</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-e80000" name="module-e80000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><i>Why you talk like that? Or the etymology of, ‘where are you really from?’</i></div> </div><p>Her next two poems were about her father, the first him being a South Side White Sox fan, and the next about becoming his caretaker due to Alzheimer's:</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-5c0000" name="module-5c0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><i>Yesterday is today. Today is tomorrow is the day before</i><br><i>Yesterday is last year </i><br><i>Tomorrow, today, and there is no more now anymore.</i></div> </div><p>Del Valle, a singer/songwriter, peppered her soulful Spanish singing between the verses of the poem about her father. “ His health really started to decline in November . . . and I was sitting in the living room with him one day and I was just kind of watching him sleep in his recliner and I just kind of started hearing a song,” she told the crowd.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“ I wanted to keep it real Chicago,” Del Valle said. “I wanted to keep it very current to what it is that I’m thinking and feeling and writing about currently.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The poems traversed a range of themes: falling in love, the lake and landscape, her family, all with a deep tie to Chicago and place.</p><p>After the Slam ended she explained to a reporter why she chose the poems she did. “ I wanted to keep it real Chicago,” she said. “I wanted to keep it very current to what it is that I'm thinking and feeling and writing about currently.”</p><p>After her performance, fans at the Slam snapped up a $2 zine she made to showcase the haikus she’s been writing daily for April’s National Poetry Month. Of course, the zines all sold out. Walking out the door, she said: “I only made 25 of them; I wish I had made 50!”</p>
entries.author: Ari Mejia | WBEZ

entries.title: These CPS students care for horses and pigs at school. They’re adding shelter puppies to their resume.
entries.summary: <p>Alan Aguirre has more experience caring for creatures with no legs, eight legs and webbed feet than those with fur and paws.</p><p>The Chicago High School for Agricultural Sciences junior has had turtles, vampire crabs, spiny eels, tarantulas and various fish as pets.</p><p>But starting Wednesday he’ll get his first taste of what it’s like to raise a puppy.</p><p>Alan and a classmate are fostering puppies as part of a partnership between the school and the nonprofit PAWS Chicago, which are hosting an adoption event at the school’s Mount Greenwood campus on Saturday.</p><p>The students volunteered to care for the puppies to get them out of the shelter for a few days and raise awareness about the event, but also to get more experience working with animals. Both students are learning how to raise livestock and other animals as part of the school’s animal science program, and hope to go into animal care after they graduate.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Alan Aguirre, 16, feeds treats to his foster puppy Prince at the Chicago High School for Agricultural Sciences. Alan is new to caring for dogs, but has a lot of experience caring for other animals at school and pets at home.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“People would say that dogs are a basic animal,” Alan said, “but it’s still nice to know how to take care of them, because I plan to work in a pet store and own my own pet store so it's a good experience.”</p><p>Alan and fellow junior Jada Toussaint met their foster puppies for the first time Wednesday as PAWS volunteers visited the school to teach nearly 60 students in the animal science program about the shelter adoption process. The students also learned tips for taking care of puppies for the first time, and what a career in veterinary medicine could look like.</p><p>The puppies pranced on a table as Alan and Jada held treats in their hands, lifting them up to the dogs’ curious noses to try and get them to perform a basic sit. PAWS volunteers stood nearby to show them how to hold the treat above the dogs’ heads so they’d rest on their hind legs.</p><p>Jada hopes to study veterinary medicine after she earns her high school diploma. She plans to attend Tuskegee University in Alabama and maybe open her own vet practice one day.</p><p>“I want to be able to manage it and also manage the business,” Jada said.</p><p>Their puppies will be among 10 that will be up for adoption at Saturday’s event. Up to 30 students will volunteer to help wrangle the puppies and guide visitors. Those interested in adopting can fill out <a class="Link" href="https://www.pawschicago.org/our-work/pet-adoption/adoption-process" target="_blank" ><u>applications ahead of time.</u></a></p><p>Katie McMahon, who teaches animal science at the school, said the partnership helps educate students about how to become responsible pet owners and the limited capacity at many shelters, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2025/07/25/pet-owner-surrender-dogs-cats-overwhelming-shelters-rescues" target="_blank" ><u>including in Chicago</u></a>. It also introduces them to possible jobs.</p><p>“There’s shelter management, there’s veterinarians, there’s vet techs and all the things that go with that,” McMahon said.</p><p>The college-prep magnet school has a unique focus within Chicago Public Schools. It’s built on a 70-acre farm where students learn how to raise and manage cattle, horses, chickens, pigs and other animals that call the place home.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>At the Chicago High School for Agricultural Sciences, seniors help to monitor the animals’ health. Some students recently helped deliver this litter of piglets.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Emmanuel Camarillo/Sun Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>As juniors in the animal science program, Alan and Jada help with labor-related duties, such as feeding the animals and cleaning their stalls. When they become seniors, their responsibilities will include monitoring the animals’ health. A group of students recently helped deliver a litter of piglets.</p><p>The school also has career-focused programs on the economics and technology of agriculture, horticulture, urban farming and food science and technology. About 760 students are enrolled at the school.</p><p>Alan said his passion for animals stems from his family in Mexico. His grandfather owned a ranch there, where he raised chickens and other farm animals. “I started falling in love with animals from that moment,” Alan said.</p><p>Jada has a similar story. She was born in Chicago but says she was raised in Mississippi around cattle and chickens.</p><p>“That’s where my love for animals came from,” Jada said. “Being around them in general and knowing the history behind them and how they work added to that love.”</p><p>Jada has helped train her family’s and friends’ dogs and also has experience bottle-feeding kittens and caring for her own deaf cat. She hopes that anyone looking to adopt a puppy or own a pet knows that they should do research ahead of time to understand the animal’s particular needs.</p><p>Alan and Jada are both excited to foster their puppies, even if it's only for a few days. Alan just hopes things don’t get messy at home.</p><p>“Hopefully he doesn’t pee on the floor a lot,” Alan said.</p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: SNAP food assistance cutoffs begin Friday in Illinois — what you need to know
entries.summary: <p>Thousands of people in Illinois are expected to begin losing federal food assistance beginning May 1 as part of an overhaul of the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program.</p><p>In February, new and expanded work requirements for SNAP participants went into effect as part of President Donald Trump’s sweeping tax bill that passed last summer. The changes call for “able-bodied” people to work or volunteer 80 hours a month — about four hours a day — to stay in the program.</p><p>This is the first year any work requirement has been in effect since at least March 2020 because the state previously had a waiver. Even before 2020, Illinois had a waiver for certain counties.</p><p>As of Tuesday, the Illinois Department of Human Services, the state agency that administers the program, estimated that 120,000 individuals were at risk of losing their benefits starting Friday and rolling out over the next several months. That’s down about 280,000 from what the state originally anticipated would be pushed out of the program as people received exemptions from the work rules.</p><h3>Why will people lose benefits in May? </h3><p>If a person is subject to work or volunteer rules and doesn't meet them, they lose benefits after three months. The new rules went into effect in Illinois on Feb. 1. Overall, if you aren’t meeting the work rules, you can only get benefits for three months in a three-year period, according to IDHS.</p><p>Congress expanded the work rules to include 55- to 64-year-olds, veterans and parents whose youngest child is 14 years or older, down from 18 and older previously.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-200000" name="module-200000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>Help for SNAP recipients</b><br><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;padding-inline-start:48px;" id="rte-3155f6e0-43f2-11f1-aa5c-b93517248f50"><li><b>Update your SNAP information:</b> Illinois created a <a class="Link" href="https://aberp.illinois.gov/screener/ABAWD?lang=EN" target="_blank" >website</a> where you can check if you meet the new SNAP program requirements. You can update your information by calling 1-800-843-6154. <a class="Link" href="https://aberp.illinois.gov/screener/ABAWD?lang=EN" target="_blank" >https://aberp.illinois.gov/screener/ABAWD?lang=EN</a><br><i>There have been problems with the state’s benefit system in recent days, impacting customer and caseworker access. Officials say that’s now resolved and is encouraging people to try again.</i></li><li><b>Find a food pantry:</b> Here’s a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2025/10/29/where-to-find-chicago-area-food-pantries-if-snap-goes-away" target="_blank" >list of pantries</a> in the Chicago area</li><li><b>Where can I volunteer?</b><br><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;padding-inline-start:48px;" id="rte-31561df0-43f2-11f1-aa5c-b93517248f50"><li>Meals on Wheels Chicago pantry offers volunteer hours. <a class="Link" href="https://www.mealsonwheelschicago.org/volunteer-individuals-formstack" target="_blank" ><u>Visit</u> this website</a>.<a class="Link" href="https://www.mealsonwheelschicago.org/volunteer-individuals-formstack" target="_blank" ><u>https://www.mealsonwheelschicago.org/volunteer-individuals-formstack</u></a></li><li>Volunteer shifts at Nourishing Hope’s pantries are available at their <a class="Link" href="https://nourishinghopechi.org/volunteer/" target="_blank" ><u>websitehttps://nourishinghopechi.org/volunteer/</u></a></li><li>Care For Friends, 530 W. Fullerton Pkwy., can be reached at (773) 932-1010</li></ul></li></ul></div> </div><p>Community groups across Chicago have been working to create volunteer opportunities for individuals to meet the requirements. The state also has a <a class="Link" href="https://serve.illinois.gov/" target="_blank" ><u>website</u></a>, <a class="Link" href="https://serve.illinois.gov/" target="_blank" ><u>https://serve.illinois.gov/</u></a>, that offers SNAP recipients volunteer opportunities.</p><p></p><h3>Can I still apply for an exemption? </h3><p>“It’s not too late,” to apply for an exemption from the new rules, said Danielle Perry, vice president of policy and advocacy for the Greater Chicago Food Depository. “You can still reach out to the state, the Illinois Department of Human Services, and talk to them about your particular case.”</p><p>Individuals should have received a notice in the mail explaining that their benefits will be terminated because they have not met the new requirements, said Gwynne Kizer Mashon, associate director for the public benefits practice group at Legal Aid Chicago.</p><p>Individuals who are working should make sure the state knows their hours and wages. Kizer Mashon said people who earn at least $935 a month in gross income after self-employment expenses are exempt from meeting the full 80-hour requirement. Because of the minimum wage laws in the area, there are individuals who make $935 in less than 80 hours a month and won’t lose their benefits, she said.</p><p>The state developed a website, <a class="Link" href="https://www.abe.illinois.gov" target="_blank" ><u>https://www.abe.illinois.gov</u></a>, where you can check if you are eligible for an exemption, which includes people certified as physically or mentally unable to work.</p><p>If an individual can show they met the work requirements or filed an exemption for April, their benefits shouldn’t be stopped, Kizer Mashon said. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a10000" name="image-a10000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> alt="Gwynne Kizer Mashon, attorney in the public benefits practice of Legal Aid Chicago, stands at the top of a staircase at the office of Legal Aid Chicago in the Loop, Tuesday." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/75faa9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F27%2F4d%2F15ee30ce4a93a5ed90ce1880956f%2Fsnapfaq-260430-03.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/75faa9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F27%2F4d%2F15ee30ce4a93a5ed90ce1880956f%2Fsnapfaq-260430-03.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Gwynne Kizer Mashon is an attorney in the public benefits practice of Legal Aid Chicago. If someone loses their SNAP benefits, they can appeal, but in some cases it may be better to reapply, she said.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>How do I join a workforce program? </h3><p>The online <a class="Link" href="https://jobreadyil.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Job Ready Illinois Workforce Program</u></a> launched in April as a way for SNAP recipients to work toward meeting the 80-hour rule.</p><p>Through a private-public partnership, the online program has two versions of the workforce program targeting different areas, said Matthew Bruce, executive director of the Chicagoland Workforce Funder Alliance. As the program expands, they plan to add material in different languages.</p><p>“It’s very much a work in progress because we launched it so quickly, but we wanted to have something — [IDHS] and the state wanted to have something — so that an [able- bodied person] who just couldn’t get to a work program or couldn’t get exempted for whatever reason, they could do this and not have a break in their SNAP eligibility,” Bruce said.</p><p>The program has videos on a variety of topics, ranging from executive skills to workplace expectations. Enrollees are expected to take part in an independent job search, and job-searching hours can count as part of the program. Each month, enrollees will receive certificates noting their hours with the program, up to the full 80 hours, or it can help a SNAP recipient who is juggling volunteer and work, Bruce said.</p><p>“We work with the workforce system all the time, and what we fundamentally see is an under-resourced workforce system,” Bruce said. “And so the fact that all of these able-bodied adults without dependents would be penalized, have their food benefits taken away from them, because our workforce system is under-resourced, and can’t serve them at the level it should be able to serve them, is really disconcerting.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e60000" name="image-e60000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/972103f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5976x3354+0+315/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbf%2Fd9%2F3c251d2948c8ba54b8ed1d54a325%2Fsnapfaq-260430-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aefebd1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5976x3354+0+315/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbf%2Fd9%2F3c251d2948c8ba54b8ed1d54a325%2Fsnapfaq-260430-2.jpg data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d839815/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5976x3354+0+315/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbf%2Fd9%2F3c251d2948c8ba54b8ed1d54a325%2Fsnapfaq-260430-2.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d839815/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5976x3354+0+315/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbf%2Fd9%2F3c251d2948c8ba54b8ed1d54a325%2Fsnapfaq-260430-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a10fb8b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5976x3354+0+315/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbf%2Fd9%2F3c251d2948c8ba54b8ed1d54a325%2Fsnapfaq-260430-2.jpg data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Job Ready Illinois Workforce Program website </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>What can I do if my benefits stop? </h3><p>“We’re going to have to fight to get people who got cut off back on, but we can do it,” said Perry from the food depository. “It’s not over even if that does happen to you and you lose your benefits.”</p><p>Understanding what to do next if you lose their benefits in May or later this year — and how to try to get them reinstated — is why Perry urges SNAP participants to stay in contact with the state.</p><p>People can appeal a termination, but in some cases it may be better to reapply for SNAP, said Kizer Mashon. An individual should be deemed eligible if they can prove they should have gotten an exemption or met the work requirement for the last 30 days, she said.</p><p>“They’ll lose those months they missed, but they’ll get back on going forward,” Kizer Mashon said. “We think there’s a fair number of people who will be losing benefits on May 1 who are losing them because they just didn’t realize that they were supposed to do something.”</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-640000" name="module-640000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>Resources to try to get SNAP benefits restored</b><br><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;padding-inline-start:48px;" id="rte-9906eab0-43f2-11f1-aa5c-b93517248f50"><li>The Greater Chicago Food Depository can be reached from 8:30 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday through Friday at (773) 247-3663 to answer questions about SNAP benefits. Participants can also schedule an in-person appointment on their <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagosfoodbank.org/get-help/benefits-outreach/" target="_blank" ><u>website</u></a>, <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagosfoodbank.org/get-help/benefits-outreach/" target="_blank" ><u>https://www.chicagosfoodbank.org/get-help/benefits-outreach/</u></a>. </li><li>Legal Aid Chicago provides free services for people challenging a SNAP termination, seeking a work exemption or applying for SNAP. Call (312) 341-1070 or visit their <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagosfoodbank.org/get-help/benefits-outreach/" target="_blank" ><u>website</u></a>, <a class="Link" href="https://legalaidchicago.org/get-help/snap-work-requirements/" target="_blank" ><u>https://legalaidchicago.org/get-help/snap-work-requirements/</u></a>. </li></ul></div> </div><p>People can prepare to challenge a termination by keeping logs showing their work or volunteer hours. Kizer Mashon said this will be particularly important for those who work odd jobs or are gig workers. A worker can also get a signed letter from the individual for whom they are doing odd jobs attesting to their hours.</p><p>To pursue an appeal, the deadline is 90 days from the date on the notice of termination issued by the Illinois Department Human Services by mail, Kizer Mashon said. The state usually tries to resolve the issue, but it could also entail multiple phone calls and exchanging documents.</p><p>Kizer Mashon recommends people seek out free legal help to appeal their case.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c50000" name="image-c50000"></a> <picture 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/988f412/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6677x3747+0+352/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe2%2F16%2F4d2463504ca28539fb8e69e0a7d9%2Fsnapbenis-110225-12.jpg"
