class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Ronald Leopold, executive director of the Anne Frank House in Amsterdam, in the recreated annex room that Anne’s older sister, Margot, shared with their parents, Otto and Edith Frank. Here, Leopold is peering at some original Margot Frank writings. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The full-scale recreation of the Frank family’s hideout during World War II in Amsterdam is the extraordinary centerpiece to the Griffin Museum of Science and Industry’s “Anne Frank The Exhibition,” opening Friday and running through early 2027.</p><p>“It is meticulously recreated. It is what it was when they were in hiding,” explained Ronald Leopold, executive director of the actual hideout in Amsterdam, which has been preserved as a museum.</p><p>Even the annex’s gloomy light, falling on stark wallpaper and narrow cots with scuffed metal frames, feels like it has been somehow time-traveled from the Nazi-occupied Netherlands. The spartan furnishings are not original but are from the World War II era.</p><p>But sprinkled among them, visitors will find, behind clear plastic cases, genuine artifacts belonging to the Frank family, including one of the diaries that Anne kept.</p><p>In a room before visitors enter the annex, they see a smiling Anne, her blue eyes radiating hope and possibility. On the walls surrounding her face: Giant projections of her diary words appear, the fountain pen ink looping across the projected page with a scratchy urgency — as if the teenager somehow knew her eventual fate.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-7b0000" name="image-7b0000"></a> data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Frank family and four other Jews in hiding spent much of their day in a room like this one. They could not risk leaving the annex or being seen inside it. The windows in this room are covered with blackout screens.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The exhibit, much of it presented on somber matte-black walls, tells the story of Anne’s early life in Frankfurt, Germany before the family, fearing the rise of the Nazi regime, moved to the Netherlands in the early 1930s. But in 1940, the Nazis occupied the Franks’ adopted homeland and began imposing ever more onerous restrictions on Jews. When Anne’s older sister, Margot, received a summons on July 5, 1942, to report for transportation to a German labor camp, the family packed up and took refuge in the hideout above a company where Otto Frank, the girls’ father, manufactured pectin (an ingredient in jams and jellies).</p><p>The Franks — Otto, Anne, Margot, and the girls’ mother Edith — would spend 25 months cooped up with four other Jews in hiding. They survived for so long with the help of some of Otto’s non-Jewish office workers.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A recreation of the secret door that led into the annex, which the Nazis eventually discovered. It is still not known whether someone betrayed the Frank family and their housemates.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In her diary — written in Dutch — Anne describes her fears, extreme boredom, her longings, hopes for the future.</p><p>“While she is being known as the face of the Holocaust, it is incredibly important for us to present her not as a victim. This was a girl who loved life, who radiated life. … She was so much more than a victim,” said Leopold, who also helped oversee the Chicago exhibit.</p><p>In Anne’s room, we see magazine clippings of Hollywood movie stars plastered on her walls. Scattered throughout the annex are objects of hope for a life after the war: a bicycle, a party dress.</p><p>And in the pages of her diary, we see Anne’s resilience: “It’s really not that bad here, since we can do our own cooking and can listen to the radio in Daddy’s office. … We also have a supply of reading material, and we’re going to buy lots of games. Of course, we can’t ever look out the window or go outside. And we have to be quiet so the people downstairs can’t hear us.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/252a182/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3a%2F6e%2F96cddf66444597d53f31840cef4f%2Fannefrank-2605030245.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A recreation of the secret door that led into the annex, which the Nazis eventually discovered. It is still not known whether someone betrayed the Frank family and their housemates.| Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>On Aug. 4, 1944, an SS officer and three Dutch collaborators arrived outside the hiding place, taking an hour before they discovered the secret bookcase door. All eight people inside were arrested and taken away. To this day, it is not known if someone betrayed the Frank family and their housemates.</p><p>Anne and Margot were taken to Bergen-Belsen, a concentration camp in northern Germany, where they both died of typhus in early 1945. Edith was taken to Auschwitz, where she died of starvation. Of the eight people inside the annex, only Anne’s father survived. In 1945, the Soviets liberated Auschwitz, and Otto returned from there to Amsterdam.</p><p>It was in that city that 70,000 Jews were taken out of their homes within a period of 16 months in order to be murdered elsewhere, Leopold said.</p><p>Otto returned to an empty annex, the Nazis having taken everything away. But before they cleared it out, friends of the Franks retrieved as much as they could, including Anne’s writings.</p><p>That annex, unlike the recreation in Chicago, is empty — just as it was after Otto returned from Auschwitz.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2e0000" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Near the end of the “Anne Frank The Exhibition” presentation, dozens of editions of Anne’s diary are displayed. The presentation also shows the letter from a New York publisher who turned down the opportunity to take on the project, one that has to date sold more than 30 million copies. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“In Amsterdam, it’s very much about absence: Who is not there anymore, what is not more anymore,” Leopold said.</p><p>Otto rarely spoke about his own pain at the loss of his family.</p><p>“He was able in one way or another to cope with his loss and to transform it into a mission for the future,” Leopold said.</p><p>Part of that mission included publishing his youngest daughter’s diary. Toward the end of the exhibit, there’s a letter from a New York publisher declining the offer to take on the project.</p><p>“It seems to me unlikely that the [diary] would have a large enough sale to cover the present high cost of production,” the publisher wrote.</p><p>Since first being published in 1947, the diary has appeared in more than 70 languages and sold more than 30 million copies.</p><p>The exhibit is free with admission to the museum. Parental caution is advised. There are, among other things, some grainy black-and-white images of mass killings of Jews.</p><p><i>For more information, go to </i><a class="Link" href="https://www.griffinmsi.org/exhibits/anne-frank-the-exhibition" target="_blank" ><i><u>griffinmsi.org</u></i></a><i>.</i></p>
entries.author: Stefano Esposito

entries.title: What's it like for local media covering Cubs, White Sox? I'm finding out
entries.summary: <p>You might have noticed a beat other than sports media under my name lately. Have no fear, I’ll still be here (I have no illusions you were concerned). But as we navigate life between baseball writers, I’ve been chipping in on the Cubs and White Sox beats.</p><p>Not only am I fulfilling a dream I’ve had since I was a kid — covering baseball — but I’m getting out and seeing the broadcast media’s coverage up close, not just from my office chair at home.</p><p>It has been fascinating (such as seeing how broadcasters prepare for games) and boring (such as waiting out a three-hour rain delay).</p><p>Let’s start with that delayed Angels-Sox game, which began Monday and ended Tuesday. Reporters are allowed access to the clubhouses before games. On Monday, the Sox’ clubhouse opened at 2:45 p.m. The unfailingly punctual person I am, I arrived at Rate Field at 2:15.</p><p>Despite a losing record, the Sox drew a crowd of media, with Fox 32 and WGN 9 making appearances. With the Cubs on the West Coast and the Bears done drafting, the Sox were the only game in town.</p><p>Sox TV analyst Steve Stone was there, too, and we chatted. He noticed that I had covered the game Sunday and offered a helpful critique of my story, pointing out my failure to acknowledge the different climate the suddenly punchless Sox were hitting in back home.</p><p>“You read that?” I asked in bewilderment.</p><p>If you heard Stone call Cubs games with Harry Caray back in the day, you would agree that he sounds exactly the same today. His voice belies his 78 years on earth. He also has the same wry sense of humor and the same prescient powers he did then. He is a marvel.</p><p>“Who do you need?” he asked me, looking around the clubhouse for someone I might want to interview. I said I was hoping to speak with Austin Hays, who had been activated from the injured list, but it turned out he wasn’t available.</p><p>So I joined the media members who were waiting to hear from manager Will Venable. About a dozen of us, including camera crews, stood in the middle of the Sox’ clubhouse and waited … and chatted … and waited. The life of a beat writer includes a lot of waiting.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-dd0000" name="image-dd0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fc0232d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5712x3206+0+539/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd2%2F89%2F1126b71b4efbae0e09effc3b4287%2Fimg-5664.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a9d52e7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5712x3206+0+539/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd2%2F89%2F1126b71b4efbae0e09effc3b4287%2Fimg-5664.jpeg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/83834ce/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5712x3206+0+539/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd2%2F89%2F1126b71b4efbae0e09effc3b4287%2Fimg-5664.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A Cubs baseball game is being shown on a laptop computer. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/83834ce/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5712x3206+0+539/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd2%2F89%2F1126b71b4efbae0e09effc3b4287%2Fimg-5664.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c09ea27/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5712x3206+0+539/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd2%2F89%2F1126b71b4efbae0e09effc3b4287%2Fimg-5664.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/83834ce/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5712x3206+0+539/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd2%2F89%2F1126b71b4efbae0e09effc3b4287%2Fimg-5664.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>As the rain fell in Chicago, I checked in on the Cubs in San Diego. I’ll be on the Cubs beat this weekend.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Jeff Agrest/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>But I wouldn’t have guessed we’d wait through a three-hour delay. I filed my two stories, downed two complimentary jumbo hot dogs — one too many — and searched for a live game to watch. The Cubs’ game in San Diego had started. I’ll be at Wrigley Field on Sunday, so I tuned in.</p><p>When the Sox’ game finally started at 9:40, maybe several hundred fans had stuck around. They were treated to a great finish — around 12:30 a.m. After hearing from Venable and slugger Munetaka Murakami, I wrote a game story and eventually left the ballpark after 1:30.</p><p>That, my friends, was a long day. But it’s life in the big leagues. Delightful, but long.</p><p>It can’t be easy on the broadcasters, either. They have to stay sharp on the air. And one of the sharpest around is Sox radio voice Len Kasper. Every day I’ve been in the clubhouse, he was there, too. No one puts in more preparation than him, and it’s evident in his broadcasts.</p><p>When Kasper was the Cubs’ TV voice and Fox would take a game, he’d show up anyway and sit with the writers. They’d look at him incredulously and ask what he was doing there. He just loves to be at the ballpark.</p><p>Navigating Rate Field for the media is easy. The spacious press box is a short walk from the elevator, which leads to a short walk to the clubhouse, which is a short walk to the dugout. Wrigley Field is a jewel, but covering a game there is a different experience.</p><p>With the Mets in town April 17, the confined press box was packed, as was the ballpark. Though Wrigley has an elevator near the press box that leads to the underground clubhouse, media aren’t guaranteed access after games. You might have to take the ramps and dodge exiting fans to make manager Craig Counsell’s presser in time. It’s still better than it was pre-renovation.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c40000" class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Jeff Agrest/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In the clubhouse before the game, I chatted with Cubs field reporter Taylor McGregor about her preparation. She talks with personnel in the clubhouse and on the field during batting practice to glean information. She also does recorded spots for the pregame show.</p><p>I complimented radio analyst Ron Coomer for the show he and partner Pat Hughes put on to get through a blowout loss to the Phillies that week. Among the topics they discussed: Coomer’s first major-league at-bat, Hughes’ first major-league broadcast (also a blowout loss), Pete Rose and Lee Smith. Coomer appreciated it but deflected praise to Hughes.</p><p>I also caught up with TV analyst Jim Deshaies, who was wearing a Yomiuri Giants cap he brought back from the team’s trip to Tokyo last year. He’s as delightful off the air as he is on it. He was telling me a story about Vin Scully when he had to leave to meet with Counsell.</p><p>I think I’m going to like the baseball beat.</p><div class="PageListC" data-hide-authors="true" entries.title: Pritzker commission investigating Operation Midway Blitz 'lays the foundation for real accountability'
entries.summary: <p>Federal immigration agents acted illegally and with impunity to carry out President Donald Trump's aggressive deportation campaign in Chicago — and they did so with steadfast support from the president and his administration, a state accountability panel found. </p><p>After spending six months gathering testimony, video footage and legal records, the Illinois Accountability Commission <a class="Link" href="https://ilac.illinois.gov/2026-04-iac-final-report.html" target="_blank" >put out its final report</a> Thursday examining Operation Midway Blitz and the alleged abuse carried out by federal agents in and around the city last fall. </p><p>Gov. JB Pritzker assembled the commission in October to investigate federal agents' conduct during Operation Midway Blitz. At a news conference Thursday, Pritzker said he assembled the panel of legal experts to conduct a thorough investigation when he realized the Trump administration wasn't going to hold agents accountable.</p><p>"These hearings and investigations demonstrated that Operation Midway Blitz was not an unfortunate collection of errors in judgment or limited abuses of power by individual bad actors," Pritzker said. "It was a carefully orchestrated, coordinated effort that started with Donald Trump and was executed by Stephen Miller, Kristi Noem, Tom Homan, Greg Bovino and others." </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d00000" name="image-d00000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9dea696/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb5%2Fe3%2F6996284547e2b64fdccdd2c58e3c%2Fimg-9407.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/23e4c5c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb5%2Fe3%2F6996284547e2b64fdccdd2c58e3c%2Fimg-9407.jpeg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2acc4cd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb5%2Fe3%2F6996284547e2b64fdccdd2c58e3c%2Fimg-9407.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Gov. JB Pritzker speaks at a podium " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2acc4cd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb5%2Fe3%2F6996284547e2b64fdccdd2c58e3c%2Fimg-9407.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c943ce7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb5%2Fe3%2F6996284547e2b64fdccdd2c58e3c%2Fimg-9407.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2acc4cd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb5%2Fe3%2F6996284547e2b64fdccdd2c58e3c%2Fimg-9407.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Gov. JB Pritzker and his Illinois Accountability Commission discuss the panel’s final report that published Thursday. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Kaitlin Washburn/Sun-Times </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p> Earlier this week, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/04/27/pritzker-illinois-accountability-commission-operation-midway-blitz-hearing" target="_blank" >the commission conducted hearings</a> and brought in witnesses and victims to speak about several of the major flash points during Operation Midway Blitz. That included the confrontations between residents and federal agents in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/public-safety/2025/11/28/border-patrol-bodycam-video-tactics" target="_blank" >Little Village</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/24/immigration-agents-arrest-lake-view-protest-tear-gas" target="_blank" >Lake View</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/26/northwest-side-ice-arrests-tear-gas" target="_blank" >Old Irving Park</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/14/crash-involving-immigration-agents-in-east-side-leads-to-tear-gas-detentions" target="_blank" >East Side</a> and Evanston. </p><p>The report also names specific federal agents the commission alleges broke the law. One of those agents is Charles Exum, the Border Patrol agent who shot Marimar Martinez five times after a car with three CBP agents collided with her vehicle on Oct. 4 in Brighton Park. Martinez and her attorney Christopher Parente <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/04/28/illinois-accountability-commission-pritzker-federal-immigration-agents-ice-midway-blitz" target="_blank" >testified before the commission on Tuesday</a> about the shooting and its aftermath.</p><p>Other agents seen in several videos aren't named because the commission lacked the power to investigate all the agents involved, said Judge Rubén Castillo, the commission's chair. </p><p>That's why the report <a class="Link" href="https://ilac.illinois.gov/content/dam/soi/en/web/ilac/documents/final-report/IAC-Referral-Letter-04-2026.pdf" target="_blank" >includes a letter</a> referring charges to Cook County State's Attorney Eileen O’Neill Burke, the Chicago Police Department, the Kane County state's attorney, and the Elgin, Franklin Park and Evanston police departments. </p><p>"It is very clear to me," Castillo said at Thursday's news conference, "that the U.S. Justice Department will not be doing any investigating of these agents who committed these atrocities. So what is left? What is left is our local prosecutors, and in particular, our Cook County State's Attorney, and she will be referred this report. She has said that she hasn't been referred anything. Well, today she's going to be referred something, and it should be investigated." </p><p>And if Burke "doesn't want to investigate," Castillo said, she should "step aside and let a special prosecutor come in and do what needs to be done." </p><p>Castillo, a former U.S. district chief judge, is <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-cook-county-oneill-burke-trump-ice-border-patrol-midway-blitz-special-prosecutor" target="_blank" >among a coalition calling for</a> a special prosecutor to investigate federal agents.</p><p>The commission lacked both subpoena and prosecutorial power. It's now up to local law enforcement to carry on the commission's investigation, Pritzker said. </p><p>"The commission has done much of what is in its power to do, to lay the foundation for real accountability," he said. "It has gathered the evidence. It has heard from innocent victims. It has identified the culprits. But it will take the courage and conviction of all of us at every level of government to continue pressing forward, to ensure that these findings are met with action." </p><p>In response, Burke issued a statement Thursday maintaining that she can't bring charges until she has received a completed investigation from a law enforcement agency. </p><p>"We commend the bravery of every witness who testified about their harrowing experiences before the Illinois Accountability Commission," Burke said in the statement. "There is no doubt that Operation Midway Blitz has traumatized and harmed our communities. We look forward to receipt of the full report and will work with our local, state, and county law enforcement to review the material." </p><p>The final report includes <a class="Link" href="https://ilac.illinois.gov/content/dam/soi/en/web/ilac/documents/final-report/IAC-FinalReport-AppendixB-Policy-Reccommendations.pdf" target="_blank" >several other recommendations</a> for local, state and federal authorities, such as: <br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-aa4dab32-44cc-11f1-856d-7183ac2ad83b"><li>The Department of Homeland Security "must [rein] in agents’ use of chemical agents, physical force and vehicular pursuits." The department should also ensure all agents wear and use body worn cameras and prohibit agents from covering their faces and hiding their identity while on the job. <br></li><li>Congress "must amend the Immigration and Nationality Act to end warrantless arrests for civil enforcement matters and prohibit discriminatory stops that rely on race, ethnicity, workplace, accent or language." <br></li><li>The state of Illinois should work with local governments and nonprofits to "alleviate the economic damage caused by Operation Midway Blitz, expand access to legal services and family preparedness planning support, and prevent further disruption to civic life by immigration enforcement activities." </li></ul>
entries.author: Kaitlin Washburn

entries.title: Funeral services announced for Chicago police officer killed at Swedish Hospital
entries.summary: <p>Funeral services for a Chicago police officer John Bartholomew, who was fatally shot at Swedish Hospital last Saturday, will be held next week in Chicago's Edgewater neighborhood.</p><p>Visitation will be held 2 p.m. to 8 p.m. on May 7 at St. Andrew's Greek Orthodox Church, 5649 N. Sheridan Rd., according to an <a class="Link" href="https://memoryeternalfunerals.funeraltechonline.com/obituaries/John-G-Bartholomew?obId=48229089" target="_blank" >obituary</a> posted Thursday. Funeral services will be held there at 10 a.m. on May 8.</p><p>“John will be remembered as a devoted family man whose life was defined by love, loyalty, and service,” the obituary said. “He was always the first to step in and help others, known for his generosity and willingness to give without hesitation.”</p><p>Bartholomew, a 10 year veteran at the police department, died after he was shot by a robbery suspect who was taken into Swedish Hospital for an exam last Saturday.</p><p>The suspect, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/30/swedish-hospital-john-bartholomew-alphanso-talley-chicago-police-department" target="_blank" >Alphanso Talley</a>, pulled out a gun and shot Bartholomew and another officer after he was uncuffed and given privacy to undress and was given a gown and a blanket in a hospital room.</p><p>Bartholomew was taken to Illinois Masonic Medical Center and died shortly after. The other officer remains hospitalized.</p><p>Talley was charged with first-degree murder, attempted first-degree murder and aggravated kidnapping.<br></p>
entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: Feds arrest 22 tied to illegal sports betting ring in Northwest Indiana
entries.summary: <p>Federal prosecutors announced charges Thursday against 22 people in connection with an illegal online sports betting ring in Northwest Indiana.</p><p>According to an 87-page indictment, James “Jimmy the Greek” Gerodemos and Dean “Dean Gem” Gialamas ran the Gerodemos Gambling Organization from January 2021 to April 2026 and allegedly used extortionate means to collect gambling debts and laundered the money.</p><p>Prosecutors said law enforcement agencies had spent years investigating the organization as part of an effort to dismantle its operations. Officials said the group exploited individuals and undercut legitimate businesses in the community during its operation.</p><p>“This was not a case of harmless or casual gambling, it was part of a broader pattern of criminal behavior that included coercion, intimidation and financial exploitation,” FBI special agent Tim O'Malley, of the Indianapolis Field Office, said at a news conference Thursday.</p><p>The organization was headquartered at Gino’s Steakhouse in Merrillville and Paragon Restaurant in Hobart, owned by James Gerodemos and Chris Gerodemos. They used the businesses to collect gambling proceeds, pay off bettors and transfer gambling proceeds, prosecutors said.</p><p>The organization also used employees from both restaurants to help collect and distribute gambling proceeds from agents, bookies and bettors. Prosecutors said the payments ranged from a few hundred dollars to as much as $50,000.</p><p>When bettors fell into debt, members of the organization allegedly harassed and threatened them and their family members, going so far as to track them down if the debts went unpaid, according to prosecutors.</p><p>“This type of activity does not remain contained,” said O’Malley. “It creates fear in the community, it creates pressure, and it impacts the entire community. Not just those who are directly involved. There is no place in our society for coercion or intimidation and those who conduct this conduct must be held accountable.”</p><p>More than 150 law enforcement officers, including the FBI and IRS, raided both restaurants and several homes during a planned operation on Wednesday and placed 22 people, including James Gerodemos and Gialamas, under arrest.</p><p>The accused are facing charges including, conducting an illegal gambling business, conspiracy to commit and attempting to commit Hobbes Act extortion, conspiracy to commit money laundering, transmission of wagering information and forfeiture allegations.</p><p>“Our goal is to completely dismantle this group,” said O’Malley.</p><p>O’Malley said agents made several arrests nationwide in connection with the illegal gambling ring, including in Indiana, Chicago, Boston and New York.<br></p>
entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: Tener acceso a un abogado es la base de la justicia y el sueño americano
entries.summary: <p>Hace sesenta y cinco años, <a class="Link" href="https://www.americanbar.org/groups/public_education/law-day/history-of-law-day/" target="_blank" >el Congreso designó el 1 de mayo como el Día de la Ley</a> para honrar el estado de derecho. Cada año, la Asociación Estadounidense de Abogados elige una temática. El <a class="Link" href="https://www.americanbar.org/news/abanews/aba-news-archives/2026/04/law-day-events-focus-on-rule-of-law/" target="_blank" >tema</a> de este año es “El estado de derecho y el sueño americano”.</p><p>El estado de derecho respalda “el sueño americano” asegurando el acceso a asistencia legal. Sin embargo, muchas personas terminan en las cortes sin un abogado, no por elección, sino por circunstancias. Esto coloca a las personas en desventaja, ya que los abogados aportan las habilidades y la estructura necesarias para presentar hechos, explicar problemas y aplicar la ley.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-6b0000" name="module-6b0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Nuestro sistema legal se basa en defensores capacitados. Cuando solo una parte tiene un abogado, o cuando ninguna de las partes lo tiene, el sistema puede no funcionar como debería. La Corte Suprema ha reconocido el papel central de la representación legal: “El derecho a un abogado es la base de nuestro sistema adversarial”. Esto se menciona a menudo en casos criminales, pero se extiende con la misma fuerza a los casos civiles (como inmigración).</p><p>De hecho, presentarse en la corte solo pone en riesgo el sueño americano. Como dijo una vez el juez Thurgood Marshall: “El simple acceso a las puertas de la corte no asegura por sí solo un funcionamiento adecuado del proceso adversarial”.</p><p>La Corte Suprema de Illinois está abordando esta brecha mediante estrategias para ampliar la disponibilidad de abogados en comunidades con pocos abogados, promover el acceso remoto a las audiencias de la corte, simplificar los procedimientos legales y reformar las reglas sobre mediación y arbitraje para resolver disputas de manera más eficiente.</p><p>Otro esfuerzo involucra a <a class="Link" href="https://il.freelegalanswers.org/" target="_blank" >Illinois Free Legal Answers</a>, una clínica virtual de asesoría legal en la que abogados voluntarios responden preguntas legales civiles en internet para residentes de bajos ingresos de Illinois. Nuestra Comisión de Acceso a la Justicia ha liderado esfuerzos para eliminar barreras que impiden que las personas utilicen las cortes de manera significativa.</p><p>En los casos criminales, donde las apuestas son más altas, Illinois ha fortalecido la representación de las personas acusadas de crímenes con estándares y apoyo consistentes en todo el estado bajo el <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/other-views/2025/05/20/statewide-public-defense-office-county-illinois-judges-christopher-wellborn-bonnie-hoffman" target="_blank" >Acta de Representación Financiada e Independiente</a> (FAIR) que estableció una oficina estatal de defensa pública.</p><p>Este esfuerzo requiere coordinación entre las tres ramas del gobierno.</p><p>La legislatura creó el marco; el poder ejecutivo apoya su operación y la Corte Suprema de Illinois está ayudando a guiar su desarrollo.</p><p>Se están realizando trabajos para alinear el financiamiento, la estructura y las operaciones en vista del lanzamiento de la Oficina del Defensor Público del Estado el 1 de enero de 2027.</p><p>Cuando las personas tienen acceso a un abogado, pueden ser escuchadas y su caso puede presentarse de manera completa. Así es como perdura el estado de derecho. Así es como perdura el sueño americano. Nada más servirá.</p><p><i>El presidente de la Corte Suprema de Illinois, </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2025/09/09/justice-p-scott-neville-jr-named-chief-justice-of-illinois-supreme-court" target="_blank" ><i>P. Scott Neville, Jr.</i></a></p><h3>Mantener los teléfonos celulares en los salones de clase por razones de seguridad</h3><p>Entiendo por qué algunos quieren prohibir <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/04/25/should-illinois-ban-cellphones-in-classrooms-during-school-hours-heres-what-you-told-us" target="_blank" >los teléfonos celulares en los salones de clases</a>. La distracción absoluta impide el aprendizaje y puede afectar las habilidades sociales. Pero con el alto número de tiroteos escolares en el país, prefiero priorizar la seguridad por encima de la distracción y no quiero que se prohíban los teléfonos celulares en los salones de Illinois.</p><p>Encuentren una manera de mantener los teléfonos celulares en el salón de clases, pero no en las mochilas de los estudiantes. Las escuelas que han tenido tiroteos no anticiparon esas tragedias, así que no asumamos que no ocurrirá. Cuarenta estudiantes con teléfonos celulares son mejores que un maestro con un teléfono si hubiera un ataque. La seguridad es primero.</p><p><i>Richard Barber, Mount Greenwood </i></p><h3>Exponiendo la desigualdad salarial en CPS</h3><p>Como maestro retirado de las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago (CPS), felicito al Sun-Times y a WBEZ por exponer los<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/04/24/trabajadores-de-cafeterias-de-cps-bloquean-la-calle-en-protesta-mientras-negociaciones-se-estancan" target="_blank" >salarios ridículamente bajos</a> que se pagan a los trabajadores de las cafeterías escolares. Sería genial mostrar algunos de los salarios de consultores de CPS, trabajos obtenidos con palanca y otros contratos para exponer la disparidad y el despilfarro.</p><p><i>Gene Jersha, Yorkville </i></p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Review: Bruce Springsteen balances songs of defiance and resistance with messages of hope and unity
entries.summary: <p>Bruce Springsteen brought his E Street Band to Chicago for a sold-out show on Wednesday night, marking the latest stop on the “Land of Hope and Dreams” tour. It was the most politically motivated pop concert hosted at the United Center since Nov. 2, 2024, when Stevie Wonder appeared during his tour named “Sing Your Song! As We Fix Our Nation’s Broken Heart.”</p><p>Wonder’s concert brimmed with hope and optimism days before the 2024 presidential election. Springsteen’s tone Wednesday was starkly different. Fifteen months into the Trump administration, the Boss arrived with songs of defiance and resistance.</p><p>Rock classics like “Born to Run” would come in due time. To begin, however, Springsteen had a message to convey.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Rage Against the Machine guitarist Tom Morello guests on guitar during Bruce Springsteen and the E Street Band’s show Wednesday at the United Center.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Our democracy, our Constitution, [and] our rule of law are being challenged right now as never before by a reckless, incompetent, radical and racist administration,” said Springsteen. “We ask all of you to join with us in choosing hope over fear, democracy over authoritarianism, the rule of law over lawlessness, ethics over unbridled corruption, resistance over complacency, truth over lies, unity over division, and peace over war.”</p><p>The E Street Band then erupted into a strident cover of Edwyn Starr’s 1970 anthem “War,” featuring former Libertyville resident Tom Morello. As a founding member of Rage Against the Machine, the guitarist is no stranger to protest rock.</p><p>The band followed with Springsteen’s 1984 hit “Born in the U.S.A.,” about a Vietnam War veteran who encounters a lack of opportunity upon his return home. The song has often been mischaracterized as a patriotic anthem. Morello and Springsteen met at center stage to trade guitar solos as the song reached its fervent peak.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2025/11/14/review-tom-morello-and-friends-chicago-performance-vic-theatre" >Review: Tom Morello and Friends provide the soundtrack for the resistance in powerful set at Vic Theatre</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Springsteen’s prior local appearance in 2023 had packed Wrigley Field to the uppermost seats. That ballpark performance featured celebratory fare like “Rosalita” and “Mary’s Place.” Those were swapped on Wednesday for the turbulent power of songs like “The Ghost of Tom Joad” and the dramatic “American Skin (41 Shots).”</p><p>Nonetheless, the United Center set list offered fan favorites. In addition to the fire and brimstone of “Murder Incorporated,” crowd-pleasers included the sing-along stroll of “Hungry Heart.” The eventual encore was elevated by “Tenth Avenue Freeze-Out” with tributes to dearly departed band members, including saxophonist Clarence “The Big Man” Clemons and organist Danny Federici.</p><p>The heart of the show reflected Springsteen’s caustic opening remarks, though. The E Street Band covered the Clash’s anti-authoritarian “Clampdown” with brash punk spirit. “Anger can be power, know that you can use it,” sang Morello. Springsteen echoed the sentiment with his rebellious “Wrecking Ball.” “Hold tight to your anger,” he sang. “Don't fall to your fears.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2da6da7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fae%2F11%2F023021aa4beca379534855a517f1%2Fspringsteen-260501-11.jpg data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c692e2e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fae%2F11%2F023021aa4beca379534855a517f1%2Fspringsteen-260501-11.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Bruce Springsteen performs Wednesday at the United Center.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Streets of Minneapolis” addressed the fatal shootings of two people by federal immigration agents in January during the Trump administration’s deportation campaign in Minnesota. Seething lyrics mentioned “King Trump,” Stephen Miller, deputy chief of staff for policy and homeland security adviser, and Kristi Noem, secretary of homeland security at the time, holding them accountable for the deaths of Renee Good and Alex Pretti. The audience joined Springsteen mid-song to shout, “ICE out now!” The crowd seemed to anticipate a connection between local resistance to Midway Blitz in Chicago last year to the events in Minneapolis, but Springsteen did not mention it.</p><p>Springsteen’s concerns extended to America’s standing abroad when introducing “My City of Ruins.” “This White House is destroying the American idea and our reputation around the world,” he said. The song was offered as a prayer for strength and courage to make things better. The soulful lamentation echoed Motown-styled R&B and gospel, featuring Charlie Giordano’s shimmering Hammond organ.</p><p><b></b> The main set closed with the unifying message of tour namesake “Land of Hope and Dreams.” While Jake Clemons echoed the bristling power of his late uncle Clarence’s saxophone, the E Street Choir quoted gospel standard “People Get Ready.”</p><div class="Enhancement" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/89472db/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F55%2F61%2Fde9d9c1b4f13a7e5590a56a6cd4f%2Fspringsteen-260501-15.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/78f5864/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F55%2F61%2Fde9d9c1b4f13a7e5590a56a6cd4f%2Fspringsteen-260501-15.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Bruce Springsteen performs Wednesday at the United Center.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The musicians put their guts into an encore featuring the irrepressible “Born to Run” with its cinematic imagery of a “runaway American dream.” Irish reel “American Land” depicted America as a utopia of endless riches for any hardworking immigrant. “Dancing in the Dark” was slyly performed under full house lights. <b></b></p><p>As the evening came to a close, Springsteen mourned the painful division between neighbors. “We’ve got a president who says he wishes nothing but ill upon those [with whom] he disagrees,” he said, adding that America was built through disagreements while recognizing others’ humanity and dignity. The show concluded with a stirring cover of Bob Dylan's “Chimes of Freedom.”</p><p>As critical as the theme was throughout the concert, the string of protest songs brimmed with love of justice, unity and country. “Someway, somehow, America renews itself,” Springsteen said. “We’ll make it through.”</p><p>Trump has publicly dismissed Springsteen’s relevance, calling him “a boring singer” and a “total loser.” Fans left United Center on Wednesday energized and ready to call that assessment fake news.<br><b></b></p><h3>Bruce Springsteen and the E Street Band set list for April 29, 2026, show at United Center</h3><p>War<br>Born in the U.S.A.<br>Death to My Hometown<br>Clampdown (The Clash cover)<br>No Surrender<br>Darkness on the Edge of Town<br>Streets of Minneapolis<br>The Promised Land<br>Two Hearts<br>Hungry Heart<br>Youngstown<br>Murder Incorporated<br>American Skin (41 Shots)<br>Long Walk Home<br>House of a Thousand Guitars<br>My City of Ruins<br>Because the Night<br>Wrecking Ball<br>The Rising<br>The Ghost of Tom Joad<br>Badlands<br>Land of Hope and Dreams</p><p><b>Encore</b><br>American Land<br>Born to Run<br>Dancing in the Dark<br>Tenth Avenue Freeze-Out<br>Chimes of Freedom<br>This Land Is Your Land (walk-off recording)</p>
entries.author: Jeff Elbel

entries.title: Republican legislators call for SAFE-T act amendments following Swedish Hospital shooting
entries.summary: <p>SPRINGFIELD — Republican lawmakers in Springfield are calling for amendments to the SAFE-T act in the wake of the fatal shooting of a Chicago police officer, allegedly by a man who had been granted pretrial release by a Chicago judge.</p><p>Alphanso Talley is accused of shooting two police officers at Swedish Hospital in Chicago. John Bartholomew, 38, was killed. A second officer was critically wounded.</p><p>Republicans have long pushed for amendments to the SAFE-T act, which abolished cash bail to give judges discretion over pre-trial release based on the flight risk posed by the accused person. House Minority Leader Tony McCombie, R-Savanna, said “this law is not working the way Democrats promised.”</p><p>Talley had a multi-year rap sheet and was awaiting trial for multiple violent charges. He was granted pre-trial release with an ankle monitor by Judge John Lyke.</p><p>Saturday, prosecutors allege, he robbed a dollar store, was captured, then taken to Swedish Hospital, where the officers were shot. </p><p>McCombie and Senate Republican Leader John Curran (R-Downers Grove) teamed up Wednesday to push bills amending the SAFE-T Act. The proposed legislation would revoke an individual's eligibility for pre-trial release if they commit another, similar crime while awaiting trial under electronic monitoring.</p><p>“Pre-trial release is a second chance," said Curran. "It is not a third, or fourth, or twelfth chance.”</p><p>Curran responded saying the SAFE-T act’s laws are too lenient and leave too much space for a judge to make a poor decision.</p><p>“In this instance, the judge would not have even been able to make that decision had we never passed the SAFE-T act,” Curran said.</p><p>Having missed multiple key deadlines in Springfield, the bills proposed by Curran and McCombie face a tough path to passage before the legislature wraps up in May.</p>
entries.author: Matt Trunfio

entries.title: Skylar Diggins enjoying 'honeymoon phase' with the Sky
entries.summary: <p>Coach Tyler Marsh doesn't have the words for it, exactly. He just knows how it feels the moment Skylar Diggins walks into the gym.</p><p>“There's an accountability and a level of focus that she demands,” Marsh said at <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky’s</a> media day. "It's contagious to the rest of the group. That's what we wanted when we brought her in."</p><p>Coming off a 10-34 season, Marsh and general manager Jeff Pagliocca knew they needed to raise the talent level on the roster. They also wanted to get tougher.</p><p>Diggins could help with both. She’s known for being an elite passer, a pesky defender and according to teammate Rachel Banham, a constant source of classic quotes in practice. But the thing that has defined her 12-year career more than anything else is that she runs a tight ship.</p><p>"There's gonna be nights when she's gonna be tough on us, and that's important," Banham said. "We need that. That's what's going to get us over the hump and into that playoff push."</p><p>Diggins grew up in South Bend, Indiana, raised by a mother who didn't let much slide. Straight A's were expected. A college scholarship was too. Strong standards, Diggins came to believe, demand respect.</p><p>So far in training camp, she's shown more than told. Diggins says the team is in the honeymoon phase — vibes are high, and the vision feels within reach. But even the honeymoon has had its buzzkills.</p><p>Three of the Sky's major free-agent additions — Azura Stevens, DiJonai Carrington and Courtney Vandersloot — are currently out with injuries, which means preseason lineups haven't yet reflected what the Sky are building toward. The Sky’s young and largely new core of Kamilla Cardoso, Rickea Jackson, and Jacy Sheldon are figuring out how to jell on the fly.</p><p>Everyone is taking cues from Diggins.</p><p>"I'm excited to still be somewhere where my opinion feels cherished," Diggins said. "But I also understand that a lot of young people are looking up to me, so I'm trying to be a good girl."</p><p>At 35, Diggins has a clear sense of what the game means to her and what she wants to pass along.</p><p>"When you've been in the game so long, when you peel off all the layers, that's really what I play for now," Diggins said. "Not for the attention, just the joy that I feel on the floor, the release that I feel on the floor and how it's like a safe haven to me. I still cherish the game and keep it very pure.</p><p>“Being in the game so long, you see what you get out is what you put in. It's like an ATM machine. That's what I try to teach the younger players. Just show them how I show up and why I've been here so long."</p><p>Diggins' decision to take a leap of faith on the Sky had layers to it. As is often the case in free agency, she relied first on the word on the street. She played with Banham at Unrivaled, who spoke highly of Marsh and his vision.</p><p>She was also drawn to the fact that two members of the 2021 championship core — Vandersloot and Stevens — wanted to return.</p><p>"I've been on the other side in 2021 playing against that championship team," Diggins said. "Seeing the arena light up. Seeing Chicago basketball at its peak. I've been here as a fan since before I was in the league. When Ticha Penicheiro signed, I came to her first game. I've always been submerged in the culture of Chicago Sky. I know what it is."</p><p>More than anything, the decision came back to her Midwestern roots.</p><p>"A lot of it didn't need to be sold because I was so close to family," Diggins said. "But also from a basketball operations side, just the moves that they were making — really putting their money where their mouth was and wanting to make some good change toward getting back competitive, in the playoffs."</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true"
